I added a new wait() function to curio that can be used to play with some of these ideas.  Here's an example::

     # Some tasks
      task1 = await spawn(coro())
      task2 = await spawn(coro())
      task3 = await spawn(coro())

      # Wait for them in completion order
      async for task in curio.wait([task1, task2, task3]):
              result = await task.join()
              print('Result ->', result)

      # Wait for the first task to complete then cancel the rest
      async with curio.wait([task1, task2, task3]) as w:
              task = await w.next_done()
              result = await task.join()
              print('Result ->', result)
      # Remaining tasks cancelled here

I might change it further.  Just experimenting for now.

Setting up a Python 2.6 development environment:
```bash
sudo add-apt-repository ppa:fkrull/deadsnakes
sudo apt-get update
sudo apt-get install python2.6

virtualenv -p "$(which python2.6)" venv-2.6
. venv-2.6/bin/activate
pip install -r requirements.pip
pip install nose
```

It looks like there are three main reasons why the tests are failing:
- String formatting requires explicitly named or numbered placeholders.
- The `gzip.open` function doesn't work in the `with` statement.

Oh, I think I finally understand what you mean now! 

Let me say this in simpler terms just to make sure I got your point :P: 

Let's say we have a dataset consisting of 4 features. You set `k_features` to 2. Now, during the "sfs," it records that the features 2,3,4 together result the best performance; it returns only 2 features (e.g., 2 & 3) though, since that's what you asked for via `k_features=2`. However, you really want this "best" combination that was discovered during the sfs.

I think that's a cool idea and a neat tweak to the original algorithm. We could add an argument like `k_features="best"` so that the algorithms goes from max to min (in backward sel.) or min to max (in forward sel.) and returns the feature subset that had the best performance during this process. > Is there a reason only Proto2 is used?

Yep. I actually started out using proto3, but I was forced to switch because I found a situation where it was impossible to serialize a message that worked properly (Google's server is proto2-based). In proto3 there's no difference between setting a field to its default value, and not setting it at all. This also means that if a field has its default value, it will not be serialized. There's at least one places in the protocol where you need to serialize a message that contains a default value.

First of all, in this case we can define new docstring with new types.

The second, - subclasses should follow interface.

Third, - it's a code smell and bad practice:

"Refused Bequest
The smell of refused bequest is much stronger if the subclass is reusing behavior but does not
want to support the interface of the superclass. We don't mind refusing implementations, **but
refusing interface gets us on our high horses**. In this case, however, don't fiddle with the
hierarchy; you want to gut it by applying Replace Inheritance with Delegation."

M.Fowler, "Refactoring".

I agree wholeheartedly!! :) It's kind of crazy that this code should be understood by static analyzers, since it contains meta classes, a lot of  `__new__`, state modifications and just a lot of other weird stuff!

I usually don't have trouble reading Python code, but this module really beats everything I've seen yet. Even Django's meta classes are nothing, haha! I guess for mypy this isn't much of a problem, since they are more on the "we're just executing everything with our custom interpreter" side. However static analysis that doesn't execute things (like pylint, pycharm and jedi) has a much harder time.

I think I just might have an idea how we could implement quite a few of these typing quirks:

- Normally __getitem__ is not allowed on classes. This works only with meta classes. We don't even have to go as far as understanding meta classes. We can just implement `Class.py__getitem__(self, index)`, check if the classes parent module is `typing` and if it is, we return our own custom class.
- Defining these new classes would be straight forward, we have our own `typing.py` module that is way simpler and has these classes:

```
from collections import abc

[...]

class Sequence(abc.Sequence):
    def __init__(self, instance):
        self.__instance == instance

    def __getitem__(self):
        return self.__instance

class MutableSequence(Sequence, abc.MutableSequence):
    pass

class List(MutableSequence, list):
    pass

[...]
```

This approach is pretty much the only solution I can think of now. It's really simple and I think could work really well. Completion would work for both `x: List` and `x: List[int]`. Also forward references could work in a recursive way: You evaluate the index from  `Class.py__getitem__(self, index)` and check if it's a string (or in case of a tuple recurse).

BTW: I have intentionally not implemented all the functions needed for MutableSequence... There's no need for implementing `__setitem__` in type inference, because there's no gain in information. The function needs to be defined somewhere, however. But this is the case in `abc.MutableSequence` (that's why the abc class is a "second" citizen, because we're using the defined methods there to minimize our efforts.)

You can change `/proc` filesystem location like this:

```
import psutil
psutil.PROCFS_PATH = "/something_else"
```

See:
https://pythonhosted.org/psutil/#psutil.PROCFS_PATH
https://github.com/giampaolo/psutil/issues/558
Please note that most information on Linux is retrieved via /proc but not all of it (e.g. `swap_memory`, `net_if_addrs`, `net_if_stats`, `disk_partitions` and others) so the PROCFS_PATH trick does not cover all psutil API.

Hello!

I'm not sure I really understand your situation (there's quite a lot of code there!), but:

* ``drf-yasg`` generates each model in ``Models`` section of the swagger document strictly from serializers - so if you have three different serializers, you should have no problem in getting three different representations generated
* the type generated for ``serializers.ChoiceField`` and ``serializers.MultipleChoiceField`` [is currently hardcoded to ``string``](https://github.com/axnsan12/drf-yasg/blob/master/src/drf_yasg/inspectors/field.py#L391) (in ``ModelSerializer``s, model fields with ``choices=...`` automatically generate ``ChoiceField``s - http://www.django-rest-framework.org/api-guide/fields/#choice-selection-fields).  
    + This hardcoding is because I found no obvious way to introspect the expected type of the ``ChoiceField``, and because strings are always accepted as input by the django-rest-framework ``ChoiceField``: https://github.com/encode/django-rest-framework/blob/master/rest_framework/fields.py#L1373. I don't know how/if this could be handled better in ``drf-yasg`` - maybe we should also convert all values in the ``enum`` key to strings? (just like DRF does when handling input)

However I wasn't really able to follow what is your desired output. Does the type/enum mismatch cause problems for you? Which part exactly would you like to be generated differently?

It would really help if you could provide the exact definitions of each of your problematic **serializers** (this can be done by printing the serializer itself: http://www.django-rest-framework.org/api-guide/serializers/#inspecting-a-modelserializer), along with what you would want to be generated differently in the swagger schema.

Ok, I kind of know what's going now. My test server was sending 16K in a single write. When I make it respond with multiple small writes of 50 bytes each, the client recv() returns 50 bytes at a time. Probably what is happening here (and I am speculating a bit as I am not intimately familiar with the SSL protocol) is that each write from server is encoded as a separate SSL record, and on the client side recv() for an SSL socket will return 1 record at a time.

It looks like the Kafka broker is sending the FetchResponse as a bunch of small writes. In fact it looks like it is doing a write for each topic name, and a write for each (empty) message set. That explains the pattern of 18 byte reads (the message sets) and somewhat larger reads (the topics). The reason I see 30 byte reads when using the master code is that it is using the newer protocol. If I specify api_version=(0,10), it goes back to 18 bytes.

So, I'm not sure there is much that can be done about getting the small chunks of data from recv(). Do you think it might be possible to safely assemble the response in a tighter loop, without going back through a select() call every time, in order to make it more efficient?

def seek_to_last():
    consumer = KafkaConsumer(bootstrap_servers=config.kafka_bootstrap_server,
                             group_id=config.kafka_check_proxy_thread_group,
                             value_deserializer=lambda m: json.loads(m.decode('utf-8')), auto_offset_reset='latest',
                             enable_auto_commit=True)

    partitions = consumer.partitions_for_topic(config.kafka_raw_proxy_topic)

    if len(partitions) > config.TASK_OK_PROXY_SCAN_THREAD_N:
        logger.error("...................")

    for partation in partitions:
        p = TopicPartition(config.kafka_raw_proxy_topic, partation)
        mypartition = [p]
        consumer.assign(mypartition)
        # consumer.seek_to_end(p)
        last_pos = consumer.end_offsets(mypartition)
        pos = last_pos[p]
        logger.info("%s,  %s" % (partation, pos))
        # consumer.seek(p, pos)
        offset = OffsetAndMetadata(pos, b'')
        consumer.commit(offsets={p: offset})

```

It's happening because you're using "safe=True" there, which ensures that PyYAML's more robust serialization is used, and de-serialized data will be same as original, at the cost of some prettiness.

Reason why that one string is singled-out for escaping is simple - only `0.51` will be de-serialized as a number (0.51), while `0.5.1` and `0.7.1` will be interpreted as strings anyway (due to two dots in there), so no need for quotes there.

If you won't be passing safe=True, you should get desired output, but when de-serialized by any sane YAML parser, it will not match the original types, as mentioned.

Example:
```
% python3 -m pyaml <<< "{'y': ['0.5.1', '0.51', '0.7.1']}"
y:
  - 0.5.1
  - 0.51
  - 0.7.1

% python3 -c 'import yaml; print(yaml.safe_load("{y: [0.5.1, 0.51, 0.7.1]}"))'
{'y': ['0.5.1', 0.51, '0.7.1']}
```
Note missing quotes around 0.51 in the last output, indicating that it indeed was parsed as a float object instead of string.

It's an intended behavior that "unsafe" (default) serializer may produce such quirks (though hopefully not many), with human-readability preferred over correctness.

Functions implemented for network, diskio and filesystem plugins in the latest commit: 5497ae1

For example, if you want to hide the loopback interface (lo) and all the virtual docker interface (docker0, docker1...):

    [network]    
    hide=lo,docker.*

For example, if you want to hide the loopback disks (loop0, loop1..) and the specific sda5 disk:

    [diskio]
    hide=sda5,loop.*

For example, if you want to hide boot mount points:

### Implementation Notes
Hello, I finally got around to wiring this up, heres a picture and some demo code:

![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/1915543/27203554-48caf4e4-521e-11e7-95b8-b5a4094d7b61.png)

Blue wires are data and clock, black is ground, and orange is power.
I have wired up the ports on TCA9548A to the RPi as follows:

| TCA pin | Remarks | RPi pin | RPi function | |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| VIN | Power | P1-01 | 3V3 |
| GND | Ground | P1-6 | 0V |
| SDA | Serial Data | P1-03 | I2C1 SDA |
| SCL | Serial Clock | P1-05 | I2C1 SCL |
| RST | Reset | P1-01 | 3V3 | Via 10K pull-up resistor |
| SD0 | to SDA on display 0 | P1-01 | 3V3 | Via 10K pull-up resistor |
| SC0 | to SCL on display 0 | P1-01 | 3V3 | Via 10K pull-up resistor |
| SD1 | to SDA on display 1 | P1-01 | 3V3 | Via 10K pull-up resistor |
| SC1 | to SCL on display 1 | P1-01 | 3V3 | Via 10K pull-up resistor |

The pull-up resistors on the slave-side SDx/SCx pins are important ... I had some 10K's hanging around and they seemed ok. The RPi already has pull-ups (1K IIRC), so they are not needed there.

The crucial bits about the program below are:
* `mux_select` picks the correct display to select - this _must_ be done before each interaction with a specific device
* normally the luma device classes will initialize an `smbus2` object internally, but since we need access to it to control the TCA9548A we have to construct it manually and pass it into the SSD1306.
* the luma device classes normally clean-up on exit, but some extra code should be employed here to clear them down by `mux_select`ing and then calling cleanup manually (haven't bothered in the example).
   
```python
#!/usr/bin/env python
# -*- coding: utf-8 -*-
# Copyright (c) 2017 Richard Hull and contributors
# See LICENSE.rst for details.

"""
TCA9548A I2C Multiplexer example

Assumes the multiplexer is on address 0x70
"""
import time
import smbus2
from luma.core.interface.serial import i2c
from luma.oled.device import ssd1306
from luma.core.render import canvas

TCA_ADDR = 0x70


def mux_select(bus, tca_port):
    assert(0 <= tca_port <= 7)
    bus.write_byte(TCA_ADDR, 1 << tca_port)


def draw_stuff(device, count, message):
    with canvas(device) as draw:
        size = draw.textsize(message)
        x = device.width - size[0]
        draw.rectangle(device.bounding_box, outline="white")
        draw.text((10, 10), str(count), fill="white")
        draw.text((x // 2, 20), message, fill="white")


if __name__ == "__main__":
    port = 1  # set to 0 for original RPi or Orange Pi, etc
    bus = smbus2.SMBus(port)

    mux_select(bus, 0)
    device0 = ssd1306(serial=i2c(bus=bus, address=0x3C))

    mux_select(bus, 1)
    device1 = ssd1306(serial=i2c(bus=bus, address=0x3C))

    for count in range(300):
        # Select first screen
        mux_select(bus, 0)
        draw_stuff(device0, count, "Screen #0")
        time.sleep(2)

        # Select second screen
        mux_select(bus, 1)
        draw_stuff(device1, count, "Screen #1")
        time.sleep(2)
        draw_stuff(device1, count, "")
        time.sleep(2)

        mux_select(bus, 0)
        draw_stuff(device0, count, "")
        time.sleep(2)
```

In theory, we could connect up to 8 SSD1306 devices like this!

### References:
* http://www.ti.com/lit/ds/symlink/tca9548a.pdf
* http://www.ebay.co.uk/itm/191927201165

Yes unfortunately dicom allows many numeric data elements to have blank values.  I don't think using zero for those is a good idea, as many values could actually be zero, so blank has a completely different meaning.  An NaN of some kind is one possible solution, as you mentioned.

For now, I have left the blank as a blank string, but it does have the unfortunate effect that one variable could be of two very different types.  This does mean that if a blank is a possibility for a particular data element in your files, then the user code should explicitly check for it.  Pydicom itself does this in a number of locations.  I think the same thing would happen with NaN, or any other solution -- there would still be errors generated when trying to do mathematical operations.

Unless someone can come up with a better idea, I think the blank is going to have to stay.  It certainly could be better documented, however, that those extra checks should be done.

To summarise some conversations that have been had about this topic via other mediums:

As far as eliottree currently stands, the output medium is append-only (writing lines to stdout) which only really lends itself to emitting an entire tree again if any part of it changed. It's also possible to implement something with a UI library like `ncurses` but ultimately both of these ideas are a nontrivial amount of work and—certainly for what my goal is—the effort is better spent building a tool that takes a different approach and leaving eliottree on its current path.

```python
from mistletoe.base_renderer import BaseRenderer

class JIRARenderer(BaseRenderer):
    def render_heading(self, token):
        template = 'h{level}. {inner}\n'
        inner = self.render_inner(token)
        return template.format(level=token.level, inner=inner)
```

### Possible slow down

I haven't looked too deeply into this but recently I've been experimenting with [yaml](https://github.com/Animosity/CraftIRC/wiki/Complete-idiot's-introduction-to-yaml) files and pyyaml and noticed that parsing yaml files that use dashes (lists) take a lot longer than without. I also noticed that there seems to be an overhead with iterating over yaml files that have many dashes which is the case of pyrestest. Though, I'm just speculating.

Take for example the [examples/minimap-test.yaml](https://github.com/svanoort/pyresttest/blob/master/examples/miniapp-benchmark.yaml) file and remove all of the dashes and replace the no-value keys with 'true' called minimap-test-no-lists.yaml

```yaml
---
- config:
    testset: "Benchmark tests using test app"

- benchmark: # create entity
    name: "Basic get"
    url: "/api/person/"
    warmup_runs: 7
    'benchmark_runs': '101'
    output_file: 'miniapp-benchmark.csv'
    metrics:
        total_time: 'true'
        total_time: mean
        total_time: median
        size_download: 'true'
        speed_download: median

- benchmark: # create entity
    name: "Get single person"
    url: "/api/person/1/"
    metrics: {speed_upload: median, speed_download: median, redirect_time: mean}
    output_format: json
    output_file: 'miniapp-single.json'
```

```javascript

On the random.choice front, you want one of two things:

choices() gives you a function that behaves like random.choice:

```python

@given(user=st.text(min_size=1), service=st.text(min_size=1), choice=st.choices())
def test_tickets(user, service, choice):
    t=choice(('ST', 'LT', 'TG', 'CT'))
```
or sampled\_from gives you a strategy for values from a list:

```python

@given(user=st.text(min_size=1), service=st.text(min_size=1), choice=st.choices(), t=st.sampled_from(('ST', 'LT', 'TG', 'CT')))
def test_tickets(user, service, choice):
    pass
```

Glad to see this happening.  A few minor notes. Section 3.2, <info>: this is used by varlib, not just MutatorMath. Varlib uses this element in order to identify which master design should be used for the default font. Transitional designs: I'd like to see some more data to support figuring out which glyphs should be substituted for which other glyph in some area of the design space. This is sort of possible right now: you can check for instance <glyph><master> elements to find out which glyphs have transitional substitutions, which works fine, but currently heuristics are needed to guess what in area in the design space the transitions should happen, based on the instances in which the transitions appear. One way to do this would be to have a element under <sources> which contains a list of transitional glyph elements. Each specifies the default glyph and alternate glyph, and contains a list of applicable axis elements. Each axis element specifies start and end of the area where the transition as effect. If this data is present, then the <instance><glyphs><glyph><master> substitution elements are superseded.

#!/usr/bin/env python3

"""
addjob2db.py

addjob2db: Perform scheduling operations

"""
from __future__ import print_function

import os
import argparse
import json
from pytz import utc
from datetime import datetime
from apscheduler.schedulers.background import BackgroundScheduler
from apscheduler.jobstores.sqlalchemy import SQLAlchemyJobStore
from apscheduler.executors.pool import ThreadPoolExecutor
from apscheduler.util import undefined

db_file = 'jobs.db'

def jobadd(scheduler, args):
    if args.func is None:
        print("ERROR: No system call specified.")
        return

    if args.type == 'cron':

        if args.id is not None:
            id = args.id

        if args.func is not None:
            func = args.func

            arg_list = args.fargs.split(' ')

        print(args)
        name     = "foo"
        max_instances = 1

        start_date = args.start_date
        end_date   = args.end_date

    else:
        print("ERROR: invalid type({}) found.".format(args.type))
        return

    print("\n")

    scheduler.add_job(func=func, args=arg_list, trigger=args.type, kwargs=None, id=id,
                      name=name, misfire_grace_time=1, coalesce=True, max_instances=1,
                      next_run_time=undefined, replace_existing=True,
                      start_date=start_date, end_date=end_date)

    print("SCHEDULER: job added")

    return


def main():
    print("\n")

    parser = argparse.ArgumentParser(description='Add jobs to JOBS DB CLI')

    parser.add_argument('cmd', help="[add]")
    parser.add_argument('-type', default='interval', help="[interval, cron, date]")

    parser.add_argument('-func', default=None, help="- interval, cron, and date")
    parser.add_argument('-fargs', default=None, help="- interval, cron, and date")

    parser.add_argument('-id', default=None, help="- interval, cron, and date")
    parser.add_argument('-name', default=None, help="- interval, cron, and date")
    parser.add_argument('-max_instances', type=int, default=1, help="- interval, cron, and date")

    parser.add_argument('-start_date', default=None, help="- interval and cron")
    parser.add_argument('-end_date', default=None, help="- interval and cron")

    args = parser.parse_args()

    print(args)

    try:
        jobstores = {
            'default': SQLAlchemyJobStore(url='sqlite:///%s' % db_file)
        }

        executors = {
            'default': ThreadPoolExecutor(20)
        }

        job_defaults = {
            'coalesce': True,
            'misfire_grace_time':1,
            'max_instances': 1,
            'replace_existing':True,
        }

        scheduler = BackgroundScheduler(jobstores=jobstores, executors=executors, job_defaults=job_defaults, timezone=utc)
        scheduler.start()

        if args.cmd == 'add':
            jobadd(scheduler, args)
        else:
            print("ERROR: Invalid cmd specified. ({})".format(args.cmd))


    except (KeyboardInterrupt, SystemExit):
        print("\n")
        pass

    scheduler.shutdown()
    print("Done.")

    return


# Start program
if __name__ == "__main__":
    main()
```

```python
from post_office.connections import connections

connections._connections.connections['default'].close()  # because it might be opened and we can't check
connections._connections.connections['default'].open()
# actual code for sending messages
...

connections._connections.connections['default'].close()
```

Proper solution is IMO add option to disable connection caching when using long running threads.

Hmm, it all still sounds pretty complicated. Custom state object for something as simple as indent managing, which pretty much boils down to tracking number of "{" brackets, which a parser is perfectly capable to handle, isn't it?
After reading your comment, and thinking about it again, I wonder if there is another solution.

I had to implement my language, and nothing nice parser-tool crossed my path. (Maybe a packrat(?) parser could have worked, not sure.) In the end I opted for a top-down parser (simple LL(1)) implemented in pure Python functions. It has 3 levels.

- Top-level is tracking of sections (`define type` enters a typedef section, `define component` enters a component definition section, and so on.) It also has sub-sections, but let's ignore that.
- Middle level is "single line parser", it takes a single line, and decides what it is. Generally, it knows the section I assume, since I coded it also top-down. Not sure how much that is needed though.
- Bottom level is "elementary words", much like a scanner providing tokens, implemented with regular expression matching, and tracking column and length for each "word". (My words were often sub-phrases like `[ ]+is[ ]+an?`, but that's just what you see as elementary word.)

Call stack is top -> middle -> bottom, of course.
You also seem to end up at 3 levels, where 'middle' and 'bottom' is a regular parser, and 'top' is a custom state object, so that looks like a pattern :p

So, as a thought-experiment, what would happen if I implement line-parsing (ie middle+bottom) as a standard parser *seeing EOL as EOF, and implement the top level as another parser that takes matched lines from the line-parser as tokens to implement the sections (or indent levels)?

In other words, I am trying to keep the indentation (or section) management in a parser by stacking it on top of a line-parser. It would work for my section language, I think except maybe for passing section information down. Of course, if you can hack a parser-tool, you can fold the section-language and the line-languages (one for each section in general) all in one grammar specification, making it much nicer.

I am not sure if indenting could be done in a parser at all without external indent-level tracking. Say you have a language consisting of '-' and 'a' only (every other character is ignored), could you write a parser that groups 'a' based on the length of '-' prefixes?
Eg
```
a
--a
--a
----------a
a
```
to `(a, (a, a, (a)), a)` ?
(Possible solution is to see each `-` as a group?)

I've been looking at this problem some more, and a few interesting things came up. MediaWiki handles it well when : # and * are placed together on one line, as they all have the same terminating string (newline). But weirder things happen when you throw ; into the mix with # or *. For example: ```` ;* foo : bar ```` I would say the "correct" or at least most intuitive behaviour is to produce this HTML: ````
  • foo
bar
```` However, MediaWiki produces this HTML instead:
foo 
  • bar
It appears that MediaWiki doesn't know how to nest this right, and makes a mess of it. It has similar behaviours with ;# as well, and ;* apparently gets treated as identical to :*. The very similar-looking ```` *; foo : bar ```` Is correctly parsed by MediaWiki, however. So when this function gets implemented, this is something that needs to be considered: MediaWiki doesn't like regular lists nested inside definition terms.

If you follow the code, a new `Account` in non-autodiscover mode gets a `self.protocol` attribute which is where the connection pool lives: https://github.com/ecederstrand/exchangelib/blob/945c7bae23d0c8800945a941d61084e5b6436893/exchangelib/account.py#L78

This `Protocol` object is created in https://github.com/ecederstrand/exchangelib/blob/945c7bae23d0c8800945a941d61084e5b6436893/exchangelib/configuration.py#L42

The `Protocol` class has a metaclass implementation that returns a cached `Protocol` instance if the service URL and credentials match an already cached cached instance: https://github.com/ecederstrand/exchangelib/blob/945c7bae23d0c8800945a941d61084e5b6436893/exchangelib/protocol.py#L152

So assuming all accounts belong to the same server, new `Account` instances should be sharing the same protocol instance. This only works within the same Python process, of course. The protocol cache only lives in memory.

The intent of the various uses of @validation_error was definitely to consolidate arbitrary underlying errors into a consistent, correct, and catchable exception class. This is a good example of some code that makes no sense, as `validation_context` is forced to know way too much about the underlying code to be effective.

As a side note, we should also make sure that `validation_error` is preserving the full child exception for inspection, in case that's important to anyone. I don't think we want to explicitly encourage that level of behavior, but I'm happy for expert users to rely on it, assuming they know it is brittle and not guaranteed.

Good, I just want to explain why it's working now and wasn't working before. If you execute ``python -c "import sys; print sys.path"`` you'll get a list of several directories on your system where Python is searching for a module when you do ``import modulename`` or ``from modulename import X``, the search is made from left to right. I believe that ``/home/lpurba/latih/python`` directory is listed before the ``/usr/local/lib/python2.7/site-packages``. So, when you do ``from redmine import Redmine`` and Python's import system starts it's search for a ``redmine`` module it stops when it founds ``/home/lpurba/latih/python/redmine.py`` and returns it to you but that is not the module that you were actually looking for. So the basic idea is that you shouldn't have modules/packages with similar names in your system. This is not a python-redmine's fault, this is how the Python's import system works and there is nothing what I can do here. We just have to keep that in mind.

As for your second question, yes, python-redmine supports ``https``, the warning that you get, i.e. ``InsecureRequestWarning`` comes from the fact that you're using the ``requests={'verify': False}``. I believe you're using this because you have a self-signed certificate on your Redmine server, if you want to disable this warning you can find how to do that [here](https://urllib3.readthedocs.org/en/latest/security.html#insecurerequestwarning). As to the exception you're getting, i.e. ``redmine.exceptions.ResourceNotFoundError``, it is raised when your Redmine server returns a 404 error. Can you access your project through a web browser via the ``https://MYREDMINE.com/projects/MYPROJECT`` ? I believe that you'll get a 404 error because that is what is returned by your Redmine server to python-redmine. Be sure to provide a valid project identificator.

Let me know if it still doesn't work and we'll continue to investigate the reasons.

@zed 
>> ..it should be up to consumer to fix his Python environment by installing win-unicode-console and enabling it (on Windows) in his sitecustomize. I.e. I don't think it is a good idea to import win-unicode-console in a consumer script.
> 
> Yes, in the ideal world `win-unicode-console` should be completely transparent for the Python code that performs Unicode I/O and `win-unicode-console` would be shipped with Python.

That may eventually happen. Note that `win_unicode_console` is rather a collection of workarounds, and a proper Python fix would make it so no workarounds are needed, so `win_unicode_console` won't be needed as well.

Meanwhile in an almost ideal world a person having troubles with Unicode in Python in Windows console would find `win_unicode_console` a fix their Python environment by enabling it in `sitecustomize`. I see no obstacles to this.

> It is too intrusive otherwise to force users to modify their global environment in a way that might break other Python scripts.

Why? `win_unicode_console` should not break other Python scripts and it intended to work as a global fix. If there are any problems that are `win_unicode_console`'s fault, it is a bug that should be fixed. One can easily change his `sitecustomize` again if there are problems. There will also be an option to use the runner to run a script with `win_unicode_console` disabled.

> `win-unicode-console` should provide useful behavior on Unix, namely: it should do nothing there -- in particular, it should avoid ImportError.

Note if you import and enable `win_unicode_console` unconditionally in your script, you are adding an unnecessary dependency. On one side, there are Windows Python users who want to use your Unicode-printing script, but are reluctant to enabling `win_unicode_console` in `sitecustomize` or using the runner, on the other side there are Unix, Mac, and other platform Python users who want to use your Unicode-printing script and have no problems with Unicode in their consoles. I don't thing we should bother the second group by `win_unicode_console` dependency just for some special-cased convenience to the first group.

At least you should do something like 

    try:
        import win_unicode_console
    except ImportError:
        pass
    else:
        win_unicode_console.enable()

and tell your users to install `win_unicode_console` by themselves. It would be also nice to inform them about the possibility of importing `win_unicode_console` in `sitecustomize` to solve Unicode console problems not only in your script, but in all scripts at once.

Regarding the locale setting problem on Unix, even if I was to solve that issue (and I don't think I'm the right person since I know nothing about Unix locales nor have I a Unix machine for testing), I would make it a separate package as it aims to solve a separate issue.

> Any additional behavior would be an enhancement. win-unicode-console mission: "enable Unicode input and display when running Python from Windows console" could be extended: "enable Unicode input and display when running Python."

Again, for that use case there should be a separate package ``fix_unicode`` that collects information about various Unicode-related fixes for various platforms, has platform-dependent dependencies, and its ``enable`` function would enable only the relevant fixes. ``win_unicode_console`` is just a package for solving one particular issue that occurs on one particular platform. I hope it's clear that the “win” in the name stands also for “Windows”.

Nevertheless, I have made changes so that ``win_unicode_console`` is as multiplatform as possible. I still think that using it as a dependency in consumer scripts is a bad idea. On the other hand, exposing the additional functionality that is multiplatform, so one can experiment also on Unix, shouldn't hurt.

Could you please import and enable ``win_unicode_console`` from the development branch in both Python 3 and Python 2 to make sure that I didn't miss any obstacle to importing it on Unix?

I think we're converging on an understanding.

The argument that it would be easier to integrate future changes by preserving the terrible format does not hold much water for me. The [code itself](https://github.com/brandon-rhodes/python-sgp4/blob/master/sgp4/propagation.py#L59) shows how little it's been changed once they got it working properly. Others ([such as dnwnr](https://github.com/dnwrnr/sgp4/blob/master/libsgp4/SGP4.cpp)) do not seem to have difficulties maintaining the code with nice variable names, and it's much easier to read. And legible code will be easier to patch if a new version comes out in 5 years.

Using the [wayback machine](https://web.archive.org/web/*/celestrak.com/software/vallado/cpp.zip), I diffed the last few versions of the [standalone c++](http://celestrak.com/software/vallado-sw.asp) code with the following findings, and the actual algorithmic diff isn't that big. Unfortunately the file structure has been rearranged which invalidates attempts at making it easy to integrate changes.

As for passing tuples around, I think we do need to keep doing that so numba knows all the [numpy types](http://numba.pydata.org/numba-doc/latest/reference/numpysupported.html). We can't pass in objects otherwise it falls back to nopython mode which isn't very fast.

Not a black box, but a box that would not be under active development, and so could be usefully kept quite close to its source material. I hadn't imagined anyone doing active development on the Python code — if the SGP4 algorithm is ever improved or expanded or tweaked, I had imagined that the improvement would happen on a team that used C or FORTRAN code, and then the result would be passed downstream back to this version of the code. In which case, the closer this code stays to the original, the easier it will be to identify and make the change, I'd thought?

But, again, there are ugly things about the code that it would be great to fix — like the huge tuples being passed back and forth — and I have no objection to tweaking code that has to be touched anyway in the course of meeting other goals!

An alternative fix that works without changes to rest-swagger is to subclass the default TokenAuthentication being used in DRF, replace the keyword with an empty string, and use that instead of rest_framework.authentication.TokenAuthentication in your settings.DEFAULT_AUTHENTICATION_CLASSES. 

See the following merged pull request:

https://github.com/encode/django-rest-framework/pull/4097

My subclass simply looks like this:

```
from rest_framework import authentication

class DjangoRestSwaggerTokenAuthentication(authentication.TokenAuthentication):
    keyword = ''
```

And that got Swagger API key authentication working for me.

You can use the code below @vladimirnani while we wait @tomchristie's input on this issue (though he wasn't aware of it until this mention) 


    from rest_framework_swagger.renderers import OpenAPIRenderer

    class MyAPIRenderer(OpenAPIRenderer):
        API_BASE_PATH = getattr(settings, 'API_BASE_PATH', '/api/v1/')
        APP_HOSTNAME = getattr(settings, 'APP_HOSTNAME', '')

    def _get_url_splitted(self, url):
        splitted_url = url.replace(self.API_BASE_PATH, '').split('/')

        url_parts = [
            x for x in splitted_url
            if '{' not in x
        ]
        return filter(None, url_parts)
     
    def add_customizations(self, data, renderer_context):
        super(MyAPIRenderer, self).add_customizations(data,
                                                      renderer_context)
        data['host'] = self.APP_HOSTNAME # not yet sure if you fixed this on 2.0.4
        tags = []
        for url, values in data['paths'].iteritems():
            methods = values.keys()
            for method in methods:
                splitted_url = self._get_url_splitted(url)
                data['paths'][url][method]['tags'] = [splitted_url[0], ]
                _operation_id = "{}_{}".format(method, '_'.join(splitted_url))

                data['paths'][url][method]['operationId'] = _operation_id
                if splitted_url[0] not in tags:
                    tags.append(splitted_url[0])
        # sort alphabetically before 2.0.4
        # data['tags'] = [{'name': x} for x in sorted(tags)]

Thanks for starting a discussion on this! My first thought is that modifying the `request` object directly is outside the scope of pyramid session libraries. Pyramid has a session hook that lets you register a session factory (a function that returns a function that takes a `request` and returns a session), which it then calls whenever someone accesses `request.session`. The session libraries do not actually add themselves to `request.session` since that's a pyramid level interface and not a session level interface.

Within the callable returned by the session factory (i.e. the function called when `request.session` is accessed) we can modify a specific `request` instance. We use this to add an existing redis callable to the pyramid registry after the first use, and you can already override how that part works (for instance, setting and obtaining multiple redis instances based on some criteria) using `redis.sessions.client_callable`: http://pyramid-redis-sessions.readthedocs.org/en/latest/advanced.html#supplying-your-own-redis-client

Otherwise you could try defining your own session factory (see http://docs.pylonsproject.org/docs/pyramid/en/latest/glossary.html#term-session-factory) to wrap one or more other session factories so you can dispatch based on request properties like the scheme) if you want to use `request.session` to manage multiple types of sessions.

Setting those alternatives aside, I think the only way to do exactly what you asked would be to add a new request method for `session_https` and any others you want that would either wrap `request.session` usage or manually use one or more session factories. More on request methods here: http://docs.pylonsproject.org/docs/pyramid/en/latest/api/config.html#pyramid.config.Configurator.add_request_method

Although a session library *could* use the configurator to add one or more request methods at config time, this is pretty far outside the scope of intended session usage and would be highly app specific. It's more common to handle that at the app level, or via a small library (possibly making use of pyramid's `includeme` so you can add it to your `includes` in place of a lower level session library). You could then have `pyramid_redis_sessions` or `Beaker` as a dependency and use their session factories directly however you wanted. Let me know if I've misunderstood or you want to discuss further though. Thanks!

@mistercrunch thanks. There is one operational regression (also heads up @aoen, @jlowin, @artwr) due to the eager creation of task instances. In its current form the scheduler will evaluate all State.NONE task instances as the scheduler does not touch the state of a task instance current and thus due to eager creation it will take more time to evaluate them. 

I'm addressing this issue in a follow up PR (almost done/ready for review), by adding a new State "SCHEDULED". This new state not only resolves the above mentioned issue, but removes the race condition between multiple schedulers for newly created tasks. See: https://github.com/apache/incubator-airflow/pull/1514

My bad, I didn't realize the docker operator was already available in the codebase, and in this case, moving it will definitely be a breaking change. 
Having the hook is more a way to factorize all docker related operations at one place (in the case you have sensors, different operators, etc...). 
I would go to have the operator and the hook in the same code area (so not putting the hook in contrib) to ease file navigation, but I'm just a newbie here so I'll let more experimented contributor answer this point :).

I think the dask test issue has been fixed, so rebasing should help.

@davoscollective - Personally the auto magic auth fallbacks in boto have bitten me several times.  Where it ends up pulling personal or work creds from my home directory when I want the other one.  I like the idea of falling back as long as the fallbacks are explicit and documented.

We currently use a forked version of S3Hook that pulls the access key and secret from login and password as well as discussed above.  That said, I modified it to fall back to login / password and still use extras first to maintain backwards compatibility.  Personally I think we should strive for the same here, but I could see the argument for best practices replacing the backwards compatible version.
@ashb I don't really mind the lines at startup. I don't periodically restart the worker at the different airflow instances that I'm running.

In general, no tool or automation should be generating `watch-del` calls; those are intended to be for the user to manage because there isn't a reasonable way to any tooling to automatically know when the user wants the watch to go away.  The watchman server can be configured to reap a watch if it has been idle for a while: https://facebook.github.io/watchman/docs/config.html#idle_reap_age_seconds.

This just means that watchman doesn't know what to call the filesystem.
watchman will still work; you can safely ignore this message.

The only reason that watchman is interested in the filesystem type is so that an administrator can control whether we try to watch on a particular filesystem type.  This is typically used with remote filesystems such as NFS or CIFS.  You can find out more about that in https://facebook.github.io/watchman/docs/config.html#illegal-fstypes

Here are some notes from the engineer who did the networked FS evaluation:

 * SSHFS: consistency and performance problems.
 * Samba: permissions problems. It's designed for Windows. You end up with every file being marked executable or other similar problems.
 * NFS with OSX as client: UID problems. OSX uses NFS v3, which doesn't have UID rewriting. The files end up uneditable or unreadable.
 * NFS with OSX as server: security problems. NFS on OSX requires poking holes through the firewall, but doesn't have its own rich security model. This makes it non-PCI-compliant.

I'm going to ask some of our C folks to help me try your patch.

Watchman is a per-user service.  The CLI spawns a server if there isn't one running for the current user.

We have this per-user (rather than a global root-owned process for the whole system) because it makes it much simpler to implement a service that has access to your files without having to deal with funky stuff like root_squash on NFS, or making rock solid authentication, or dealing with the fsuid on linux.

We run as a daemon rather than as an in-process library for two reasons:

1. The daemon can be persistent and understand changes that happen outside the lifetime of tools, such as mercurial, that are not running persistently.  This can help accelerate them.
2. The daemon can consolidate watches for your user across multiple tool invocations.  For example, mercurial and Buck can share those watch resources and reduce the amount of kernel memory required to manage large directory trees

This model is similar in some ways to tmux or screen which have long running per-user processes that can be addressed via a socket in a well known path.

```python
import zmq.asyncio
import zmq
import asyncio
import os
import pickle
import concurrent.futures

class Terminator:
    pass


class SocketCtx:
    def __init__(self, socket_type, high_water_mark, socket_name, connect_method_name):
        """
        Creates a ZMQ socket context object.  This class will send `high_water_mark` + 1
        `ZMQEmptyMsg`s to clear out the local cache since the LINGER parameter seems to
        be ignored: https://github.com/zeromq/pyzmq/issues/832

        :param socket_type: type of socket
        :param high_water_mark: high water mark
        :param socket_name: name to pass to connect_method
        :param connect_method_name: either "bind" or "connect"
        """
        self._zmq_ctx = None
        self._socket_type = socket_type
        self._socket_name = socket_name
        self._high_water_mark = high_water_mark
        self._connect_method_name = connect_method_name

    async def __aenter__(self):
        self._zmq_ctx = zmq.asyncio.Context()
        self._socket = self._zmq_ctx.socket(self._socket_type)
        self._socket.RCVHWM = self._socket.SNDHWM = self._high_water_mark
        getattr(self._socket, self._connect_method_name)(self._socket_name)

        return self

    async def __aexit__(self, exc_type, exc_val, exc_tb):
        if self._zmq_ctx:
            self._zmq_ctx.destroy()  # will close all sockets associated with context and (theoretically) linger until all data sent
            self._zmq_ctx = None
            self._socket = None

    async def recv(self, *args, **kwargs):
        return await self._socket.recv(*args, **kwargs)

    async def recv_pyobj(self, *args, **kwargs):
        data = await self._socket.recv(*args, **kwargs)
        pyobj = pickle.loads(data)
        return pyobj

    async def send(self, data):
        return await self._socket.send(data)

    async def send_pyobj(self, obj):
        pyobj = pickle.dumps(obj)
        return await self._socket.send(pyobj)


socket_name = 'ipc:///tmp/foobar'
def sender():
    loop = zmq.asyncio.ZMQEventLoop()
    asyncio.set_event_loop(loop)

    async def doit():
        async with SocketCtx(zmq.PUSH, 2, socket_name, 'connect') as zmq_socket:
            data = {y: {x: 140 for x in range(200)} for y in range(100)}

            total_sent = 0

            try:
                for i in range(25):
                    await zmq_socket.send_pyobj(data)
                    total_sent += len(data)
            except Exception as e:
                print(e)

        print("total sent:", total_sent)

    loop.run_until_complete(doit())


async def receiver(connected_event: asyncio.Event):

    async with SocketCtx(zmq.PULL, 2, socket_name, 'bind') as zmq_socket:
        connected_event.set()

        total_received = 0

        while True:
            try:
                pyobj = await zmq_socket.recv_pyobj()
                if isinstance(pyobj, Terminator):
                    break

                await asyncio.sleep(0.5)
                total_received += len(pyobj)
            except Exception as e:
                print(e)

    print("total received:", total_received)


async def main(loop):
    connected_event = asyncio.Event()
    recvr_task = asyncio.ensure_future(receiver(connected_event))
    await connected_event.wait()

    executor = concurrent.futures.ProcessPoolExecutor(1)
    await loop.run_in_executor(executor, sender)
    async with SocketCtx(zmq.PUSH, 2, socket_name, 'connect') as zmq_socket:
        await zmq_socket.send_pyobj(Terminator())

    await recvr_task


if __name__ == '__main__':
    _loop = zmq.asyncio.ZMQEventLoop()
    asyncio.set_event_loop(_loop)

    _loop.run_until_complete(main(_loop))

Here is an example to get a build url of a firefox 64 bits linux build:
```python
import re
import taskcluster

re_firefox_linux64 = re.compile(r"firefox.*linux-x86_64\.tar.bz2$")

index = taskcluster.client.Index()
queue = taskcluster.Queue()

route = "gecko.v2.mozilla-central.nightly.2016.01.04.latest.firefox.linux64-opt"
task_id = index.findTask(route)['taskId']

artifacts = queue.listLatestArtifacts(task_id)["artifacts"]

for artifact in artifacts:
    if re_firefox_linux64.search(artifact['name']):
        build_url = queue.buildUrl('getLatestArtifact', task_id,
                                   artifact['name'])
        print(build_url)
```

The closest thing to "future-state" observation is this:

```python
checked = 0

class X(HasTraits):

    a = Int(1)
    b = Int(10)
    c = Int(100)

    @observe("a", "b", "c")
    def abc_increase(self, proposal):
        global checked
        if not self.a < self.b < self.c:
            print(checked + 1)
            raise ValueError("%s < %s < %s is not true" % (self.a, self.b, self.c))
        checked += 1
        

x = X()

with x.hold_trait_notifications():
    x.a = 10
    x.b = 100
    x.c = 0

print(checked) # '1'
```

However, if no error is encountered, the change notifications are not squashed and the observer will check state for each trait assignment (i.e. it will check 3 times).

Ok, let me explain it in more detail.

Suppose FBX node N has two meshes A and B and a child node M.

In FBX, if N has geometric transformations (GeometricTranslation, GeometricRotation or GeometricScaling) than these transformations have to be applied only to its directly attached meshes A and B, but not to its child node M.

Currently, during FBX import geometric transformations seem to be treated the same as local ones (LocalTranslation, LocalRotation, LocalScaling), so they are applied both to directly attached meshes and all children nodes.

So the way that `boto3` works is that if those values are not in the actually HTTP response that comes back, it will not include them in the dictionary returned back. It does not autopopulate missing values. So even if the value is documented, there is no guarantee that it will be included in the response. It only documents all of the possible values that the response could contain.

You can also confirm that API Gateway is not returning those values in the HTTP response by running your script with `boto3.set_stream_logger('')` right before making the API call.

We're generating Rust code from the JSON service definitions.  To write correct Rust code we translate classes into Pascal case.  `errorMessage` and `ErrorMessage` thus get translated into the same class and we end up with duplicate definitions for that class.

Right now @adimarco has modified our codegen to have an intermediate collection that discards duplicates, then outputs the generated code.

At first glance it appears the botocore JSON service definition could be simplified by using a single `errorMessage` type instead of two that boil down to the same `string` type.

I was able to find a work-around to get the request out to S3. Using the s3_client's `generate_presigned_post` I was then able to use `requests` to do a post to s3. I believe the issue with just using `s3_client.put_object` is that GAE intercepts the outgoing request and forces it to use `HTTP` instead of the required `HTTPS`. Using `requests` the request still gets intercepted but it's turned into a `urlfetch` call which allows `HTTPS`. The Working code is:
```python
# Create some of the various supported conditions.
conditions = [
    {'x-amz-server-side-encryption': 'aws:kms'},
    {'x-amz-server-side-encryption-aws-kms-key-id': '<aws_kms_key_id>'}
]

# Retrieve the args for the presigned post.
fields = {
    'x-amz-server-side-encryption': 'aws:kms',
    'x-amz-server-side-encryption-aws-kms-key-id': '<aws_kms_key_id?'
}

s3_client = Session().create_client(
    service_name='s3',
    use_ssl=True,
    aws_access_key_id='<AWS_ACCESS_KEY_ID>',
    aws_secret_access_key='<AWS_SECRET_ACCESS_KEY>',
    config=Config(signature_version='s3v4')
)
post_args = s3_client.generate_presigned_post(
    '<my-bucket>', '<key>', Fields=fields, Conditions=conditions)

# Make sure that the form can be posted successfully.
files = {'file': ('<key>', '<content>')}

response = requests.post(
    post_args['url'], data=post_args['fields'], files=files)
assert response.status_code == 204
```

Ok so the reason this is happening is thus:

`request.invoke_exception_view` only sets `request.exception` and `request.exc_info` *if and only if* it generates a response for the exception. The idea is that if you have a response object then you can check `request.exception` to deduce that the response was created on behalf of that exception. If it failed to generate an exception response then it simply re-raises the exception instead.

So the contract in a tween is that you either have an exception (`try: except:`) or you have a `response` and `request.exception`.

Anyway, what's happening right now is that the toolbar is catching the exception, rendering a response, but not setting `request.exception`. So nothing above the toolbar such as pyramid_retry can determine that the response is from an exception.

The appropriate fix is to have the toolbar set `request.exception` and `request.exc_info` in the `except` block when `intercept_exc` is truthy.

First I could verify that `_ofs` is indeed a negative value just from checking the error message we see. `None` is not a possible value here.

There seems to be exactly one place where the offset can become negative, which [is here](https://github.com/Byron/smmap/blob/master/smmap/mman.py#L133). I have uploaded [an smmap branch](https://github.com/gitpython-developers/smmap/tree/gitpython-issue-251-debugging) that might help getting notified exactly at the time the `_ofs` value turns negative.

When I see `At times` in one of your first sentences, I am thinking of data races immediately. However, I also believe that this isn't happening in your case as you certainly wouldn't even dare trying to use gitpython like that. After all, it's not threadsafe at all.

Another thing I thought could happen is the test-system running out of resources, which might have (accidentally) triggered an alternative code path. It appears to me though that the code which [allocates an memory map](https://github.com/Byron/smmap/blob/master/smmap/util.py#L166) would just trigger an exception outright.

## What could you do right away ?

1. Avoid the custom gitdb implementation by instantiating your `git.Repo` objects with `git.Repo(path, odbt=git.GitCmdObjectDB)`. This will have performance implications though, either to the better or the worse ;).

The stack trace isn't just similar to #531, it's nearly identical... finding the same offending character in the same position. I think your report explains the issue there, and here's what I think was happening:

1. pip was installing packages and in doing so installed rpl 1.5.5 (or similar).
2. When pip got around to installing setuptools, that was the first package that happened to invoke pkg_resources,
3. which triggered the failure you've reported above.

I suspect the issue is that the offending package wasn't properly packaged (or was packaged with an old or defective build system).

I'm not sure we want to patch this by suppressing the error and masking the installed version. I could see augmenting the error to help the user better trace the issue to the source.

I'll dig a bit more into rpl to see what we can learn from it.

Bat files are not always treated as executable by Windows. It's quite possible that the PATH search isn't finding the bat file. (And that's an OS limitation, not a limitation of ```py.exe```.

I'd be curious to know if ```#!/usr/bin/env python3.bat``` worked. Sometimes Windows accepts bat files, but won't automatically add the extension.

Of course, to solve your actual problem, you can just use ```#!/usr/bin/env python``` (and simply don't try to run the script when Python 2 is your active Python) as a workaround. Or explicitly specify the full path to your virtualenv Python.

1. Create a `.pex` file out of dependencies and include it in the topology JAR with a standard name like `topology.pex`. Rather than calling `/virtualenv/topology-venv/bin/python -m streamparse.run <class_name>` to run a component, we actually call `topology.pex -m streamparse.run <class_name>`. Since `pex` supports `-m` similar to a Python interpreter, this should "just work".  This seems like the preferred option -- my only concern here is a "platform build mismatch" issue, e.g. if a dependency is a C extension module that needs to be built for the target platform rather than the development platform, building the .pex file locally may not produce the right thing (?). This might not matter as much if a bdist exists for that module and pex's `--platform` argument is used.

2. We build the .pex environment "dynamically" upon topology startup, using the `pex` CLI tool. That is, rather than bundling topology.pex inside the JAR, we actually bundle the dependency list in `requirements.txt` format. We then make the topology entrypoint `pex -r requirements.txt -m streamparse.run`. This ensures that the environment is built on the remote server upon topology startup; the main downside is that this command will probably take a long time to run the first time (before a pip cache kicks in?) and I'm not entirely sure how friendly Storm will be to that. I wonder if invoking it once upon topology submit via Fabric could be a trick to warm up the pip cache while also catching requirement specification errors at submit-time rather than topology run-time.

Whichever option we pick, it seems like it could offer some improvements to the venv approach, but I haven't dug into pex and tested it out too much yet.

As implemented in the most recent commits, I propose to rename the old pre-PR 'reflect' to 'mirror' for consistency with ``ndimage``.  The behaviour for 'reflect' will become what is proposed in this PR and would be consistent with ndimage (but consistent with mode 'symmetric' of ``np.pad`` & ``skimage.util.pad``).  

Several other functions in ``skimage`` already accept both 'mirror' and 'reflect' modes (via calls to ``ndimage`` functions) .  For example:
https://github.com/scikit-image/scikit-image/blob/master/skimage/feature/corner.py#L60
https://github.com/scikit-image/scikit-image/blob/master/skimage/transform/pyramids.py#L48
https://github.com/scikit-image/scikit-image/blob/master/skimage/filters/_gaussian.py#L28

The proposed 'reflect' behavior is also what is implemented in dask's array ghosting (used by ``skimage.util.apply_parallel``):
https://github.com/ContinuumIO/dask/blob/master/dask/array/ghost.py#L301-L333

However, this still leaves the question of which is the desired behaviour for mode 'wrap'.  The proposed behavior matches ``np.pad`` and ``dask`` and is more intuitive to me, but the previous behaviour matches ``ndimage``.

Here is a summary of the current behavior in this PR:
![all_modes_v2](https://cloud.githubusercontent.com/assets/6528957/8581002/e53130c6-258b-11e5-8f43-d9678d7e2856.png)

I've tested adding in a global minimisation algorithm (`lapjv`) and tested it on the ORB and Brief examples. 

The bottom line is, it performs worse than the current brute-force local minimisation algorithm, because while it almost always returns more matches, the matches are of poorer quality. 

The reason for this is basically that the keypoints only have a relatively low overlap between the two images, so using the brute-force algorithm with `cross_check` set to `True` is a much stricter criterion than trying to globally optimise the assignments. 

**Question**
With that in mind, should I simply close this issue and not add `lapjv` matching (which btw is also much slower than the local approach), or would it be useful to include it anyway for reference and/or because cases could possibly arise where it does perform better?

Regarding your Travis failures, you can click through (under Details) to see whether the failure is related to your code or not. In your case it is: I guess you are not running doctests locally, because it is the doctest that is failing:

More broadly, thanks for your contribution, but I'm sad to say it needs a lot of refinement. I'd actually gotten started on this (thanks for your earlier issue report!) but was taking long because the "right" way, with proper deprecations, is quite tricky. Here are the goals I have for the structure tensor 3d code:

1. Use plane, row, column coordinates instead of xyz, as described here: http://scikit-image.org/docs/dev/user_guide/numpy_images.html#coordinate-conventions
2. Use a unified function (`structure_tensor`) for both 2D and 3D. See for example the code for `hessian_matrix`.
3. Have consistent code between 2D and 3D.

To satisfy those goals, we need to deprecate the current 2D xy approach in favour of row/column (rc) coordinates. There is a "model" PR for converting Hessian matrix to 3D with unified code implemented here:

https://github.com/scikit-image/scikit-image/pull/2194/files

and a deprecation of the 2D xy code here:

https://github.com/scikit-image/scikit-image/pull/2327/files

Essentially this is the path that I want to take with the structure tensor. Feel up to it @scott-trinkle?? Sorry if this is a bit overwhelming, but I am very happy to help you through the process! Thanks for CCing me on this thread, Steven.  Really a big fan of this idea.  Here are some issues that come to mind when deciding to use the IPython widget framework:

1.  The widget API is in a state of flux, and may eventually be taken out of IPython's repo completely and put in a separate repo.  The API is likely to change in the middle of your dev cycle, and it can be a pain to keep up.  I'd recommend establishing an IPython clone that everyone developing for the GUI uses during dev.

2.  Linking widgets to traits takes more boiler plate than building a GUI in TraitsUI, so there's a bit of a learning curve.  I hope that future versions if IPython will simplify the way `link` is used to connect widgets to traits.

3.  IPython uses Traitlets, not the full Traits library, so some important traits are not available.  For example, a useful widget might be a list that stores images in a `TableEditor`(http://code.enthought.com/projects/traits/docs/html/TUIUG/factories_advanced_extra.html#tableeditor), so users could go back and forth easily with a selected image.  IIRC, `List` traits are not part of Traitlets, and I'm sure the TableEditor isn't.  Most likely, Traitlets will limit the complexity of widgets and editors.  Offhand, I wonder if you could use full Traits on the Model side instead of Traitlets, or is Traitlets baked into the widgets?  This would add Traits as a dependency, but well worth it IMO.

4.  When desiging the IPython notebook GUI, we found that keeping separate Model and View classes was not too hard.  This is reflected in our classes `specmodel.Specmodel` and `specgui.SpectralGui`.  Feel free to pick through the source of these classes if they're helpful.  https://github.com/hugadams/scikit-spectra/tree/master/skspec/interact/ipynbs  Additionally, Nicholas Bollweg from the IPython core team helped us a great deal to get this up and running.  He is a whiz on designing widget GUIs and I'd recommend sending 'hard' questions to him.

5.  The ImageViewer, be it in QT, NBWidgets etc..., would probably benefit from binaries.  I think the reason ImageJ is so popular is because of how out-of-the box it's applet is.  Whether one frontend framework or another would be more suited to making executables might be an important factor to consider now.

I refactored this PR into separate branch https://github.com/max-arnold/django-cities-light/commits/feature/speedups. There are some some differences though:

* reset_queries is here, but used only in DEBUG mode
* connection.close() is called only for MySQL
* worst case with progressbar is not optimized (left as is), but it is disabled by default

My angle is that I do not want extreme optimizations which increase code complexity (there are already too many IF statements and the command is quite monolithic) and lead to regressions (memory leaks, broken connections on MySQL). At the same time, best case should be quite close to what @FaBay89 wants. Also I do not care a lot about initial import speed because we now have cities_light_fixtures command for fast and repeatable imports.

Below is my completely unscientific benchmark (ran only once, huge variation of parallel disk activity etc...):

```
stable:                           1179,28s user 28,34s system 92% cpu 21:49,57 total
removed travis:                   1167,87s user 25,26s system 91% cpu 21:46,49 total
disabled debug and reset_queries: 1123,67s user 21,87s system 92% cpu 20:39,04 total
disabled progressbar:             993,14s  user 20,95s system 90% cpu 18:43,77 total
disabled connection.close:        961,46s  user 21,34s system 91% cpu 17:48,74 total
lists to sets:                    507,23s  user 11,64s system 86% cpu 10:03,06 total
```

I plan to refactor cities_light command in the future and discussed possible approaches with James on EuroPython 2016 so it may be possible to speedup it further. As for now, I'd like to minimize extreme optimizations which can complicate planned refactoring.

I discussed that with @tarekziade and we have use-cases where we know that an update per day is enough and that the proxy should not call the Kinto server more than once a day for a specific collection.

To begin with a configuration endpoint that could define the Cache-Expire value for a given collection would be enough.

This value should define two headers:

### If deactivated:

 - ``Cache-Control: no-cache``
 - ``Expire`` current server date

### If activated

 - ``Cache-Control: max-age=<number of seconds>``
 - ``Expire`` expiration date

It is interesting to note that the client can also send a ``Cache-Control`` header to force refresh.

See http://www.w3.org/Protocols/rfc2616/rfc2616-sec14.html#sec14.9 for more information.

We don't actually *want* a mechanism to cache the responses, because we already have one with the Etags. Closing the issue for now.

I ran some benchmarks and there is one thing you can do to speed up the example. Write multiple entries as part of the same transaction. That is, move `with env.begin(write=True) as txn:` outside of the for-loop:

```python
with env.begin(write=True) as txn:
    for i in range(N):
        datum = caffe.proto.caffe_pb2.Datum()
        datum.channels = X.shape[1]
        datum.height = X.shape[2]
        datum.width = X.shape[3]
        datum.data = X[i].tobytes()  # or .tostring() if numpy < 1.9
        datum.label = int(y[i])
        str_id = '{:08}'.format(i)

        # The encode is only essential in Python 3
        txn.put(str_id.encode('ascii'), datum.SerializeToString())
```
Saving 10,000 images of size (3×224×224 bytes) took 6 seconds, as opposed to 110 seconds using the original version. Not sure if it's a good idea to make transactions huge, so you might want to try buffering them.

Yes, I do think you are correct, the problem is that normalizing the data throws everything out of whack when shorting is involved. We can't use the absolute value of the short positions and still have it make sense. 



Consider a portfolio with 1x leverage that is equally long and short. They sell 50% of the account value and get the cash proceeds, then buy assets with the other 50%. That means the account has 100% of the account value sitting in cash still, but some of it is collateral for the short sales. That cash earns interest and is an important component of a long/short strategy, especially in a high interest environment.



I we lose that when normalizing the long/short/cash components because they should equal 200% of the account value if using absolute values. 100% in cash, then 50/50 in long/short stocks.



Having this return the dataframe defined on line 59 before the normalizing would be more correct IMO. I don't think the area plot is the best thing for this either. 



Here are a few plots of a long/short strategy without normalizing the data, the area plot is not very nice, but the density plot, box plot, and histogram capture the distribution of allocations nicely.

Maybe I could explain a little bit the workflow we have. We have a react app built through Webpack. Font-Awesome is installed through npm, and the `.scss` file is imported from the app root. Then there's a webpack url-loader for the woff that inlines the the font in our css bundle. Woff being supported by all the browsers we support, it's really the only one we need. The 27kb difference with the woff2 file is not significant.

Problem is, since the woff2 is listed, browsers that support woff2 will download the file since it's preferred to woff, even if the woff is inlined.

So having the ability to update a variable and only link the formats we need would help support such a scenario, and offer some flexibility.

We need to decide if we want to support `async` and `await` as non-keyword identifiers for people trying to parse Python 3.6 and below (and, if so, how).  My inclination is that we need this support, especially because astroid now depends on us.

As for how we might go about doing this, I see two options:
1) Keep the current ast3 parsing code and use it to parse Python 3.6 and below (when requested by the `feature_version` argument to `parse`.)  This means the structure of the AST returned by `parse` could eventually differ by `feature_version`, which is not great.
2) Undo the changes in https://github.com/python/cpython/pull/1669 in typed_ast's parser to bring back the Python 3.6 behavior and add an explicit check to disallow the use of `async` and `await` as identifiers in Python 3.7 and above.

I think option 2 is the clear winner here.

Formulas are here to compute the values of the variables.

In the manuscript Ines mentioned that:

> The E. coli model used to use a fake gene s0001.

My response was: 

> That's a bit cryptic in my opinion. Ideally it'd be something verbose, as to avoid confusion and to make it immediately clear. Moritz suggested using SBO terms as a means of namespace agnostic detection of the Biomass reaction. I think it could work here too, although I am a big fan of having human readable tags

And I agree @cdanielmachado, we'd need to be more concise than just naming every reaction 'spontaneous', but I disagree with moving this check to the summary/ statistics category. After all, the majority of metabolic reactions are catalysed enzymatically, and thus we'd lose this level of control. 

Here's what I propose:
1. Check for GPR rules
2. If none are present check for the following annotation or SBO term to be present: 
- `known to be spontaneous`
- `known reaction, but unidentified mechanism`
- `gap-filled for one reason or another`
- `pseudo-reaction necessary for modeling`
- `lumped reaction`
- .. (potentially more criteria the community comes up with)
3. Assert that a reaction has either of the two above
4. If not, tally up all the reactions that aren't clearly defined and fail the test
5. If the origin of all reactions in the model is clearly defined the test is passed.

> (I found this in the wild when trying to fetch data I had sent as "normal" numeric values).

JavaScript has no 64 bits integers but only one number type, which is the IEEE754 double precision float.

So the JSON spec can't support int64 values. See the [discovery spec][1] to see how values actually get encoded.

[1]: https://developers.google.com/discovery/v1/type-format

虽然我不是专业搞这个的，但我感觉这篇文章总的来说是非常水的。首先要说的是这不是个期刊论文，而是个会议论文，只是八股文格式齐全，让人看着很是高大上。我由衷怀疑压根就没有人认真审过这篇稿子。

算法本身暂且不论，研究貌似就是用了个比较流行的库，随机森林我也不太懂，没能力验证实现的正确性。咱就说最重要的研究方法和数据统计。

测试集分为使用SS和不使用SS两个组。使用SS这一组的数据来自于一个唯一的设备，并且是和训练集相同的设备，虽然取了10次，但这10次显然不是独立的，而是有很强的相关性（比如可能每一次都在做着同一种操作，例如都在看视频，都在刷推等等），这就好比考试的时候出的都是书上原题，成绩显然不可靠。而非SS组则来自26个不同的主机，他们在干的事情可以完全不同。而这些机器当时究竟都在干什么，文章压根没提。所以从样本的选取就大大的有问题。
再说统计分析，但凡学过高中统计的都知道，用“正确率”（accuracy rate）来描述分类的效果是远远不够的，因为这个数值和真值在样本中所占的比例直接相关，假如按文章中说的，训练集 36 个样本，其中 10 个真，26 个假，我搞一套“算法”，不管什么输入，永远给出否定结论，那么不用任何训练，我的正确率就能达到 26/36 也就是 72%。而要是在实际生活中，假如全网有5%的人用shadowsocks，我的正确率就能达到95%，好像效果很好嘛……
再说这个数据的可信性。看这俩折线图，先不论有多不规范，咱就先说训练集是0的时候正确率竟然是0？正确率可能是0吗？瞎猜的正确率也该是50%吧？如果正确率是0，换句话说只要把分类标准倒过来那么正确率就是100%，也就是说不用训练就能达到100%的正确率。那我还费那个事训练干嘛？直接就无敌了呗……好吧，你说文章的 accuracy rate 不是我说的意思？反正全文都在用这个词，我没看到文中对这个词给出任何一个定义，而且一个二分类问题，不管怎么定义，正确率也不应该是0%。
最后说说这图的样式。图画得难看点能理解，但你这纵座标最高120%是几个意思？图上的点既不标置信区间也不画盒子，图注都懒得写一句。Fig.3 更是语焉不详，横座标是测试集数据的大小，训练集的数据多大？这个问题都不知道，列一堆正确率数值有啥用？

所以综上所述，这篇文章的方法就是调用个现成库，研究设计存在重大缺陷，统计玩得稀烂，数据还不可信，结论竟然敢说是“remarkable effective”，结尾竟然还有致谢，我估计这俩博士知道致谢里提到了他们准能气晕过去……

I figured out a way to get YAML anchors working in CloudFormation templates. Sweet! Here's how:

You cannot include YAML anchors directly in your primary CF template (the one you are directly deploying). Doing so will yield: "An error occurred (ValidationError) when calling the CreateChangeSet operation: Template error: YAML aliases are not allowed in CloudFormation templates"

However, you *can* use YAML anchors in imported template snippets. Then, in the deployable primary CF file, you include them as follows:

```
Resources:
  'Fn::Transform':
    Name: 'AWS::Include'
    Parameters:
      Location: !Sub s3://xxx/MyYamlAnchoredResources.yaml
```

Anchors allow CF templates to become radically smaller.

> As you probably have noticed, I've tried to do my first TDD tip, let me know whether this is how it should be done.

I'm not sure what tip you're talking about. Is it perhaps in a different branch than the pull request? Only commits on the PR branch will show up here.

The first step would be to consolidate your documentation into the first commit. An easy way to do that is check out the first commit as your TDD branch (maybe named 'tdd'), then pull in the .rst file from the last commit (with something like this):

```bash
git checkout spike docs/dev/analysis/features/bookmarks.rst
```

(This assumes the spike branch is called "spike".) This command pulls the bookmarks.rst file from the spike tip and brings it into the current working space, including all changes you've made to it along the way. Then you can amend that first commit with:

```bash
git commit --amend
```

And then rebase your spike branch onto the TDD branch. During the rebase, any changes to that file in any of the later commits will go away, and the `docs: ...` commit will have the latest. That will then be your TDD tip.

Next, you form the `acpt: add scenario...` commit for your first acceptance test(s). These need to be marked with the @wip decorator. This commit immediately follows the `docs:` commit, then you rebase the spike branch onto it.

Wherever the acceptance test first fails (when running with --stop (on first error) flag) tells you which unit test to develop first. I can take another look when you've gotten that far.

The global preference order can be determined by the order in which plugins are registered, or imported in https://github.com/imageio/imageio/blob/master/imageio/plugins/__init__.py.

If we put the tifffile import beforr the freeimage, it should be fine.

How automatic format selection works: when a file should be read or written, imageio asks each format (a plugin provides one or more formats) whether it can handle it, in the order that they're registered. The first format that says it can do it, gets to do it. Formats can look at file extensions but most also inspects the first few bytes.

The user can also provide the format explicitly as the second arg to `imread` or `imwrite`.

So IIUC you propose to allow users to specify the plugins based on extensions too? I suppose we can do that, though I am not sure how necessary it is; for tiff we should just make tiffile the default, and if people *really* want to use freeimage, they can.

> I think these link relationships make sense, especially the second option focusing on the TimeGate which links to several Mementos.

The second one is my personal preference as well. It off-loads the version resolution to the IPNS and does not pollute the object graph itself. However, the first approach was something that I put here just for comments as I was cooking the possible architectures on the whiteboard. Unnecessary chain of updates is undesired in the the first approach because the TimeMap, the node that keeps pointers to all the mementos would be changed after every memento addition, that will cause unwanted dangling versions of the TimeMap object itself that wont be connected from anywhere. Additionally, reference from the `/about/{URI}` to the TimeMap node will change as it's digest would be different. At the same time, if we decide to add a reference back from the memento node to the corresponding TimeMap node, then all the mementos would need update which would be a disaster.
