This is getting to the edge of my knowledge so please treat the information below with some caution...

The BlueZ architecture is split between the Linux kernel and user space. 
The raw HCI socket connection used by aioblescan is controlling the Linux kernel directly
The DBus API allows the user space (through bluetoothd) to control the kernel.
bluetoothd adds a lot of flow control and status tracking to assist users.
If devices are discovered with raw HCI socket commands then my _assumption_ is that bluetoothd does not get updated to know about them

There is another BlueZ API called Bluetooth Management interface (mgmt) that operates in the user space but is more HCI socket like.
It is not possible to access the mgmt API using Python. The problem seems to be that the current Python socket module doesn't let you get at it. The relevant code appears to be in makesockaddr()
in CPython's socketmodule.c - see 
https://github.com/python/cpython/blob/master/Modules/socketmodule.c#L1276.
It only ever seems to set the dev and bdaddr members, leaving the hci_channel field set to 0 (HCI_CHANNEL_RAW).

There is a presentation called "Bluetooth on modern Linux" that has more information about the BlueZ architecture:
slides: https://elinux.org/images/8/89/Janc.pdf
video: https://youtu.be/tclS9arLFzk

My theory of what's going on:

The sequence of events are:

1. A long running run bundle is started
2. The page is re-rendered after the ajax call returns
  - refreshWorksheet will cause the focusIndex and focusSubIndex to both be the end, moving the focus to the last element in the table
  - worksheet_items renders once, table_items renders once, then worksheet_items renders a final time
3. Because of the way Mousetrap works, the command that binds with a key last is the one that's used. In this scenario, for the 'up' key, that's the binding in worksheet_items.jsx (not the one in table_items.jsx, which is the one we desire) because of the previous order of the rendering. (this order of rendering has been confirmed through console.log statements).
4. Finally, hitting 'up' causes the incorrect mouse event to fire, causing the focusIndex to switch from 0 to -1 and breaking the indices.

Why is there a 'wall' for how much the user can move down?
- If step 4 were changed so that the 'down' key was the first key hit after refreshing, then a "wall" would appear because an improper focusIndex is set. 

Ideas of solutions:

It seems like the root of the problem is that the improper key bindings are being executed. My proposal is to move all Mousetrap calls into a singleton which is context aware (i.e. checks the current React state, then makes a decision on which callback to execute). 

These are the files that use Mousetrap:
- worksheet_items.jsx
- worksheet_item_interface.jsx
- table_item_interface.jsx
- worksheet_interface.jsx
- ws_mixins.jsx

you could do something like:

`"family": { "afi": "bgp-ls", "safi": "bgp-ls" }` to be RFC compliant. The confusion comes from the fact that bgp-ls is a bit special in the sense that the same nlri has multiple `types`, which I don't think happens with other NLRIs. In the code, link, prefix and node bgp-ls nlris are registered as different NLRIs, but you could use the same name and short name for all three, leaving the CODE unique so they can be registered correctly. 

Where I think `bgpls-prefix-v6`, `bgpls-node` etc could be really useful is  to replace
` "ls-nlri-type": 3`,  `"ls-nlri-type": 2`, etc, which is kind of cryptic and requires knowledge of the rfc to know what it means. so you could change that to `"ls-nlri-type": "bgpls-prefix-v4"` etc.

If we could factor-out the "WAMP protocol statemachine and associated bits" into its own library -- or just namespace inside Autobahn -- including fixing up Autobahn to use this under the hood that would be a huge win!

This is what I mean by "sans-IO design" above -- a library that JUST does WAMP processing. So then, this could be rapidly plugged into a native gevent-supporting library/API while also being used inside Autobahn. A PR that "just" factored out the WAMP protocol bits of Autobahn while keeping the Twisted/asyncio code largely unchanged sounds to me like a thing we could merge. It wouldn't even need to be a separate library (yet?). This should make a bunch of the unit-tests shorter (and make it easier to write tests for code that's not currently covered).

You can look at the Hyper/h2 library for an example of these styles of libraries working together -- the core "protocol" library (hyper-h2) is used by Twisted's HTTP2 implementation but can also be used by blocking/threaded libraries. Mostly, this would be a further refinement of Autobahn (like the one that factored out this txaio library ;). I think this would be the most likely way to build a WAMPv2 gevent library while sharing code with Autobahn

> Regarding forgetting error_if_generator or whatever, no that's up to the library authors (e.g. Autobahn) -- users (of the library) wouldn't need to remember that. This lets library authors decide which Deferred-returning functions are "special" (e.g. user-facing) instead of just "mysteriously" affecting every Deferred produced by anything using txaio.

Yes indeed, but just remember all those issues with logging in crossbar and autobahn (this also being one btw). Library authors after all are also just human beings. My point on this is, that we should design for the most common case and make the least common an exception, not vice versa.

I haven't opened an issue in Autobahn yet, because I believe this should be solved in one common spot, namely txaio. Otherwise I would have to create issues for any other user-defined async method used by autobahn as well.

I can understand your ressentiment against solving this in `as_future` but then txaio should atleast provide an easy to use `error_if_generator` function. This is not something only Autobahn needs but almost any code dealing with twisted or asyncio, the only exception being functions which actually expect a future/deferred to return a generator, which clearly make up the minority.

Yes, https://github.com/crossbario/autobahn-python/pull/748 may be "a" stop-gap solution, but we need some tests for this and also need to figure out the implications of not using the `txaio` stuff -- that is, most of Autobahn uses `txaio` so that we can have mostly the same code across Twisted and asyncio. The problem here is that this `loop=` code bypasses that.

Several options:

 - only use the `loop=` loop
 - if no `loop=` is passed, use `txaio`'s loop (via `txaio.config.loop`) if one is configured
 - just always use `txaio` directly (i.e. no bare `Future(..)` use, switch to `txaio.create_future()` like elsewhere). This option would imply removing the `loop=` kwarg to these factories, IMO, which may be a compatibility problem.

```
On 13/10/15 10:37 AM, David McLean wrote:
> Hi Alexandre, 

Hello David,

> If I do no include the group by then no tags are present in the response
> and therefore filtering inside get_points doesn't work. Is this intended
> behaviour?

Absolutely, and this has nothing to do with influxbd-python. The InfluxDB server does not return tags if you don't ask it to.

However, this can be misguiding because there was a recent change to the InfluxDB API:

  The implicit GROUP BY * that was added to every SELECT * has been
  removed. Instead any tags in the data are now part of the columns in
  the returned query.


The "tags" that you see in https://github.com/influxdb/influxdb-python/issues/251 are actually columns!

The fact that tags are now part of the columns makes if impossible for us to differentiate tags and columns :(!

> My changes don't work when "group by" includes the tag values so I'm not
> sure that my contribution is working correctly? 

It should work when using "group by", I cannot break that.

What I am getting out of this is that there are no bugs in influxdb-python. To get things working like you want, all you have to do is request the right tags by using the "GROUP BY" keyword.

So, there are two types of tags:
 - top-level tags that are returned when using group-by. Filtering works with these.

 - column-level tags. Filtering does not work with these, as they are new.

However, it looks like one would want to filter by the tags that are now included in the columns. What do you think?

Once again, I think that this discussion should be on GitHub so that everyone can see, I will post this here: 
 - https://github.com/influxdb/influxdb-python/issues/251

thank you for your work David

Best regards,

--
Alexandre Viau
alexandre@alexandreviau.net
```

> ~~the strategy we discussed is still required and valid~~

Strike that.  It doesn't work out very well.  Nevermind whether the timeouts and/or failures have to do with DNS or actually writing to an external log service.  If attempts to drain messages are failing or timing out for any reason, our goal was to more aggressively manage the queue depth by spending progressively _less_ time attempting to drain each message as the queue starts to back up.  The problem with this, however, is that spending less time on each means we're actually increasing the tempo of actions that will probably fail _and_ increasing the likelihood of continuing failure since something that's timing out already won't magically start succeeding with a _lower_ timeout.  The net effect is that this strategy doesn't really help us to recover at all.  It can make things worse, in fact.

What _can_ be done, however, is we can stop dialing so much.  Dialing the drain's URL once per message is a pattern that was imported from the old log implementation.  It has the benefit of catching DNS changes quickly if they should occur, but other than that, constantly closing an re-opening connections is really inefficient.

I'm going to update the drains so that they reuse each connection 100 times before dialing again.  This is vastly more efficient, but reusing a connection _only_ 100 times means we're re-dialing frequently enough that if DNS changes, it's never _too_ long before we're dialing the updated address.  I've already tried this with the UDP drain and it's working nicely.  TCP might be a little harder, but I'm working on it.

From experience, some of the things I was asked for when I worked on PaaS were:

- App (Container) stop, start, restart events (Probably the same as Controller Telemetry)
- Access (e.g HTTP 2XX) and Error hits on apps (Containers)
- Frequency of various platform errors, like routing failures

I guess I figure we have four classes of telemetry data users:

- Business Analytics: Desire to know information that indicates customer engagement.
- SLA data: Monitor availability for uptime and service level agreement.
- Operations: The health of the system is paramount. Errors are important.
- Security/performance: Looking for ways to identify and improve the system.

Most are interested in the same baseline set of facts, I'd imagine.

This was a design decision. tsfresh will not tinker with the input time series data by for example imputing values or dropping NAs.

Reason behind this: In data science projects, NAs should be handled with special care, often they contain a lot of information. We don't want our packages to silently remove those informations by dropping it. This is way we are not imputing the input data.

It is an edge case, so should be rare unless intentional. My main concern is with the "intentional" part: it's not about what `web3` implementations are willing to support, but what is going to be "fed" to them, in some cases maliciously.

-----

Very OT:

Yes, I'm talking about LLL mainly. :)

The earliest workflow for me was:

1. write a program where the selectors are pre-calculated and [placed as "constants" in code](https://gitlab.com/veox/lll-contracts/blob/0e7b93e48aabe091488266d930ea9bf43fbe38cd/contracts/greeter.lll#L18);
2. write a matching interface description of it [in Solidity](https://gitlab.com/veox/lll-contracts/blob/0e7b93e48aabe091488266d930ea9bf43fbe38cd/contracts/greeter.solidity), and generate an [ABI spec in JSON](https://gitlab.com/veox/lll-contracts/blob/0e7b93e48aabe091488266d930ea9bf43fbe38cd/contracts/greeter.lll.abi) using [an utility script](https://gitlab.com/veox/lll-creation-patterns/blob/e96a727ffe02b2c1e7ab1d6180ca1255d1b999c0/utils/gen-abi.sh);
3. write [tests](https://gitlab.com/veox/lll-contracts/blob/0e7b93e48aabe091488266d930ea9bf43fbe38cd/tests/test_greeter.py) that use the ABI spec from step (2) and the compiled bytecode from step (1), basically populating `contract.abi` and `contract.bytecode{,_runtime}`.

These days, the order is reversed. So, I do use `solc --hashes` to generate the same selectors as everybody else, and I place these selectors in the program, - but that's just a convenience, for the sake of compatibility with existing tools.

This is all very flexible, precisely because there are no assumptions of, say, the `bytecode` having been produced by the same tool as the `abi`.

> OR does web3 plan to support ABI generated by such languages?

For now, everyone's doing just fine pigeonholing themselves into what's already supported. No need to spread thinner. :)

-----

Rabbit-hole OT:

According to the `web3.0` zeitgeist, a program would not have both:

``` solidity
// selector collision: both 0x00000000
function left_branch_block(uint32);
function overdiffusingness(bytes,uint256,uint256,uint256,uint256);
```

because Solidity does not allow collisions in function selectors; the ecosystem is optimised for the most popular language, Solidity; "a contract" mentioned in an abstract statement will likely be Solidity. Such a program is _inconceivable_!..

An LLL program would check the length of `CALLDATA`, plus _maybe_ the first four bytes of it, and run some macro based on those checks. So, it's definitely conceivable.

Where this is going I don't know, but I'm sure that a general-purpose computer will always present ways to dig ourselves in deeper. :D

I had the same issue.
I tried installing a miniconda on top of my conda installation (as suggested in other cases of conda malfunction), but it did not fix the problem.
I had a look at the code mentioned in the traceback and came up with the following workaround.

You have to fix the timestamps in the .json files under conda-meta in your anaconda installation.
The code appears to expect an integer number, but some files have a float (actually the decimal representation of them).
The following line finds such files:
```find /home/ucac008/anaconda3/conda-meta -name "*.json" | xargs grep "timestamp.*[0-9]\+\.[0-9]"```
(replace `/home/ucac008/anaconda3` with your anaconda installation dir)

With an editor, open each of the files and fix the timestamp by removing the decimal point and the digits after it.

In my case, this fixed the issue.

There appears to be two distinctive camps for this.

Maybe that should suggest a .condarc switch, keep_root_on_activate.

There's a related issue here that we need to fix separately. If I've understood the directory scheme correctly, packages in the pkgs directory aren't namespaced in any way by the channel they come from. Currently, without the fix here, packages in a given environment can be overwritten when a different environment installs a package from a different source that has the same name, version, and build number. After this PR, the old version is kept. As far as that goes, we're exchanging a very hard to find bug for one that is at least somewhat easier to track down. A more complete fix for that would be really good, though it's definitely out of scope for this PR.

#334 fixed the problem that VUnit stopped when there was a parse error using the internal parser inhibiting the real compiler from giving a better error.

The VUnit parser is just regex based and will be worse in the presence of syntax errors in general. VUnit has no real need for a complete parser and developing one takes a lot of time and would be less robust during development. This is why we let the real compiler handle files where the internal parser failed.

Maybe this specific instance of the general problem of the regex based parser being worse can be fixed.

How did you create the repository?

The repository must be a shared file system, usually NFS for Linux systems, but s3 and HDFS are also available. Each master and data node must be able to both read and write to this shared file system, so with NFS this also requires matching user is permissions. 

The repository must be registered with elasticsearch via an API call _and_ the `path.repo` set to the shared file system mount point. In your case it seems that you've defined the path.repo as a sub folder in your regular elasticsearch data path. This would only work in a single node cluster, as that is the only way a local path is shared by all nodes, since there's only one. It defeats the purpose of a backup, too, since the storage is local. 

I'm not sure what your aiming for, but you'll need to figure out how to mount NFS (or some kind of shared fs) in your Docker containers to do snapshots.

@drcrook1 the issue you are seeing is indicative of a cross tenant authentication problem. Basically, the user you are authenticating with is targetting AAD tenant A, but the resource (ADL account) you want to access exists under AAD tenant B. This is not supported (as evidenced by the invalid token tenant message). 

This is an issue in two scenarios:
1. The user you are authenticating with only exists under AAD tenant A.
2. The user you are authenticating with has an entry in both AAD tenant A and AAD tenant B, but is defaulting to tenant A when targeting the 'common' tenant.

For the first scenario, you must add an entry for the user into AAD tenant B

For both the first and second scenario, you must initiate your authentication by targetting the specific tenant ID of the tenant that contains the ADL resource, instead of using the common tenant:

token = lib.auth(tenant_id = '**Tenant ID of AAD tenant with ADL resource**', username = user, password = password)
credentials = UserPassCredentials(user, password, **tenant='tenant ID of AAD tenant with ADL resource'**)

I agree that it is confusing that in one case you get a generic "permission error" and the other you get a more robust exception. I will work on getting the messaging consistent across these clients, since the underlying error should be the same.

It's worth considering the underlying reason why each is needed (IMO):
1. timeouts: for limiting potential delays (in this scenario almost always network because interrupting CPU is hard/expensive in cpython)
2. tracing: for tracking where time is spent (network/cpu)

the commonality is that both want to know/control the areas that can take a lot of time in aiohttp...so this this scenario they're very much alike, they want to know/control similar areas.

also, 1) can impact 2) given it can interrupt a trace.  2) similarly can impact 1) if the tracing callbacks take too long.  

so I guess the question is:
1) is one a superset of the other (does one want to strictly interact with more than the other)
2) or, do they share a similar "base" where they can diverge over time (they want to interact with similar areas, but each has different areas they want to interact with)
3) Or are they completely divergent, with no overlap of areas of interest.

I think obviously it's not 3.

Regarding `feels_empty`, if this line

```python
feels_empty = shaped and len(shaped) == 0
```

was instead

```python
feels_empty = shaped is None or len(shaped) == 0
```

then it would make more sense. I've amended the commit to reflect what I think the code has been trying to accomplish.

Presumably the intent has been that `feels_empty` should be `True` for empty containers. Well, it hasn't because `bool([])` is obviously `False`. The `serialize_when_none` setting has been filtering out empty containers not because they "feel empty" but because the containers have been programmed to return `None` when `serialize_when_none` is `False`.

This is now fixed so that the containers could just as well return `[]` or `{}` and still be affected by `serialize_when_none` at the model level.

You probably know this but just to reiterate: the intended workflow for overrides is that you hack in the external repo that you're using as an override (probably side-by-side with your main project repo), and that your main project's build scripts call `peru sync` for you automatically. The main benefit of this over in-place hacking is that peru never gets confused about which files it owns vs which files contain your changes. Say you're working on your dependency, and then you `git checkout` to an older version of your project and run `peru sync`: if you're using overrides peru is happy to fetch old versions of other dependencies while maintaining your override, but if you've edited dependencies in-place peru will refuse to sync to avoid wiping out your changes.

I can see this getting annoying in at least a couple of circumstances. 1) If your project has no build step. Then it sucks to have to run `peru sync` every time you make changes. But it might make sense to sync from your test script or whatever. 2) You want to use your editor's fuzzy-finding smartness to hop between files in your project and files in dependencies, by setting the CWD of your editor to the root of your project. Side-by-side sucks for that.

If really want in-place editing, there are a couple things you can try: You can take the approach of just editing files directly in your project, and then using `cp -r` to bring your changes over to an actual clone when you're ready to commit them. (That's probably annoying, but it's functionally about the same as the "reverse override" you mentioned, @questor.) Or just drop an actual clone in-place in your repo and work with that. In both of those cases, `peru sync` will not work anymore, unless you use `--force`, in which case it'll clobber your changes. If you want to keep working in-place and keep using `peru sync` and you're willing to get *super fancy*, you can drop in a clone and use `peru override add` to make that clone *it's own* override -- you'll still need to use `--force` though, and there might be other confusing side-effects that I haven't thought of.

At the end of the day though, peru's just not designed with this workflow in mind. Peru wants `sync` to be a guarantee that you've got what you asked for in the `peru.yaml` file. Otherwise, it needs to be an error. There's not really a difference between the accidental changes that peru is trying to prevent, and the intentional changes that an in-place workflow makes. This goes with the general philosophy that if different projects are frequently changing together, as opposed to giving each other stable APIs, they should really live in one repository.

Can't really speak for peru, but mr has a .mrconfig file to describe the
repo relationships. Thing is you can also just define whatever command you
want in there too. So for example, if I wanted to support git flow in mr, I
just add it into the config:

[DEFAULT]
flow = git flow $@
[root]
checkout = git clone ssh://git@m <//git@shaun.xbookyq.se:7999>y.git.repo
/root.git
[root/⁠module1]
checkout = git clone ssh://git@ <//git@shaun.xbookyq.se:7999>my.git.repo/
module1.git
[root/⁠module1/submodule1]
checkout = git clone ssh://git@ <//git@shaun.xbookyq.se:7999>my.git.repo/sub
module1.git


Just to be clear, the `checkout` command is a built in, `mr` just requires
it to be defined since it could be svn, hg or whatever running.

From that point on I could type `mr flow init` in the root repo and it
would do it in all 3.

Hi @sudarshan85. The short answer is that you can do everything by manipulating the `BibDatabase` `entries` attribute that is meant to be public. You should not call the `_add_entry` from the parser, this is a private method specific for the parser.

More precisely, if `b` is your `BibDatabase` object you can:
– look for an entry in: `b.get_entry_dict()['your-citekey']`,
– get the number of entries in: `len(b.entries)`,
– add one as: `b.entries.append(the_entry_from_the_other_bibdatabase)`.
When adding entries, you are however responsible for it to be well formatted (which is the case if you take it from another database).

Beyond that, one might consider improving the `BibDatabase` object for easier manipulation. It would be adding a bit of complication to the code, so I don't know how much this should be considered. Similarly it would probably make more sense to have `entries` be a dictionary but, once again, does it worth breaking the API?

I see where you are coming from.  My reason for including 'cisco' in the designation was to aid in searching and differentiate it from other unrelated 'spark' packages.  Just run ```pip search spark``` sometime and you will see what I mean.

I certainly wouldn't want to compete or misrepresent the package, as the Cisco 'official' SDK.  I talked with the BU before starting this project (I had already written one Spark API SDK, why start another one if the BU is going to release and manage their own), and long-story-short they didn't have any immediate / short-term plans for releasing a Python SDK.  My hope here was that if we could get a good community effort going here on DevNet with well structured and complete code, it could perhaps become the basis for a BU managed SDK in the future.

I am certainly open to name suggestions here.  However, I can say that so far I don't really like any of the permutations as much as I do the current naming.

- pysparkwrapper

- pysparksdk (I don't hate this one, but I don't really like it either)

I don't realy like 'client' (especially for the API wrapper package) because we aren't providing a Python-Spark client, just essentially the API's modeled as simple and native Python objects.

Brad,

Thanks again sharing your code on how you implemented this functionality.  I had to modify it slightly due to some funkiness that I saw with the 'content-type' associated with the file when doing the multi-part encoding.  _For example:_  When uploading a text file, with the ```'content-type'``` set to ```'image/jpg'```, the text file wouldn't display in the Spark client properly.

I did a little  searching and found the Python [mimetypes](https://docs.python.org/2/library/mimetypes.html) library module that has a ```guess_type()``` function that will attempt to guess the mime-type based on the file's extension.  It's probably not perfect, but it seems to do a good job, and I have set a default of ```'text/plain'``` if the ```guess_type()``` returns None.

This functionality is implemented in v0.3, which has been posted to PyPI.  Check it out and please let me know if you run into any issues.  See the [release notes](https://github.com/CiscoDevNet/ciscosparkapi/releases/tag/v0.3), for example code on uploading local files.

@pganssle Ok, understood. Actually, I want to take `dateutil` lib as en example and implement its parser in a different language (for educational purposes mostly). Therefore, I though that maybe there is a formal description of parser implementation or its grammar which could make things more simple for me.

@jbrockmendel So this is not 100% solved since there are still cases where it raises `ValueError`, and worse yet, it's not really clear when `ValueError` vs. `InvalidDatetimeError` vs. `InvalidDateError` vs. `InvalidTimeError` would be raised, which makes me think that it will be hard for people to make meaningful use of the different error types, and kinda muddies the waters.

Can you clarify what your thinking was with these error types?

I think there are two very reasonable ways to handle this:

1. Use a single error class derived from `ValueError` like `ParserError` or `UnknownFormatError` or something and always raise that. If we want to distinguish between error types based on the failure mode, we can use `raise from` so that those error types are visible, but since that is not something I think people would be able to (or want to) catch and try to recover from, it's probably sufficient to just use `ValueError` for the internal errors and wrap them for the final (catchable) error.

2. Use multiple error classes depending on the type of failure we got in a way that *could* be recoverable. I think there are approximately four possible failure modes here:
    1. We have something that doesn't look like a date (e.g. `Scramulax`)
    2. We got something that *looks* like a date, but isn't a valid date (e.g. `2017-02-30`)
    3. We got something that looks like a date with a time zone, but we don't know how to map the time zone into something meaningful.
    4. We got something that contains a date, but also contains other non-date stuff and `fuzzy` is not active.


I like the finer-grained controls of the second option *except* that there's a better than average chance that we'll be raising errors, so it won't be reliable enough to actually catch in an automated way. For example, since the parser really tries to give the input the benefit of the doubt, it will likely identify way too many things as option 2-2 when it's actually 2-1. Similarly we're already having a lot of confusion between 2-2, 2-3 and 2-4. Not to mention 2-4 is a situation that can be easily remedied by just parsing *all* dates with `fuzzy_with_tokens=True` on and then doing whatever "recovery" you need by inspecting the returned tokens.

I think maybe we should start with number 1 and maybe add some `ParserError` subclasses if in the future it actually makes sense to do so.

@DavidMuller I suspect that either you are using pytz incorrectly or this is a bug in pytz.  Note I can reproduce the bug without using anything from dateutil:

```python
    >>> from datetime import datetime, timedelta
    >>> from pytz import timezone

    >>> pacific_tz = timezone('America/Los_Angeles')
    >>> toff = timedelta(days=7)
    >>> dtstart = pacific_tz.localize(datetime(2014, 10, 27, 9, 0))
    >>> dtstart.tzname()
    'PDT'
    >>> (dtstart + toff).tzname()
    'PDT'
```
Using `dateutil.tz`:

```python
    >>> from datetime import datetime, timedelta
    >>> from dateutil.tz import gettz
    
    >>> pacific_tz = gettz('America/Los_Angeles')
    >>> toff = timedelta(days=7)
    >>> dtstart = datetime(2014, 10, 27, 9, 0, tzinfo=pacific_tz)
    >>> dtstart.tzname()
    'PDT'
    >>> (dtstart + toff).tzname()
    'PST'
```

I am no expert in pytz, but I believe the reason for the difference is that `localize()` is attaching a fixed time zone (PDT) based on the local time of `dtstart`, whereas the `tzfile()` returned by `gettz` is calculating the local time zone on request for each date. I suspect that if you want to use the `.localize()` method, you have to call it on the output of the `rrule`, not the input, e.g.:

```python
    >>> from datetime import datetime, timedelta
    >>> from dateutil import rrule
    >>> from pytz import timezone

    >>> pacific_tz = timezone('America/Los_Angeles')
    >>> dtstart =datetime(2014, 10, 27, 9, 0)
    >>> rr = rrule.rrule(rrule.WEEKLY, count=2, byweekday=rrule.MO)
    >>> rr_res = [pacific_tz.localize(out_date) for out_date in rr]

    >>> rr_res
    [datetime.datetime(2014, 10, 27, 9, 0, tzinfo=<DstTzInfo 'America/Los_Angeles' PDT-1 day, 17:00:00 DST>),
     datetime.datetime(2014, 11, 3, 9, 0, tzinfo=<DstTzInfo 'America/Los_Angeles' PST-1 day, 16:00:00 STD>)]
```

Hope that helps!

You could ignore a column on your schema by using the attributes `load_only` and `dump_only`:
```python
class SomeModelSchema(ModelSchema):
    class Meta:
        model = SomeModel

    id = fields.Integer(load_only=True)  # will not dump id field
```

The beauty of *marshmallow-sqlalchemy* is that it tries to guess all columns from your Model class, so you don't need to repeat yourself in the Schema class. If you want to specify each column to be dumped in the Schema, it's better to go with pure [marshmallow](https://github.com/marshmallow-code/marshmallow) to define your Schema.

I've not tried yet, but looking at the code at https://github.com/pyserial/pyserial/blob/master/serial/serialposix.py, line 469, it looks like that the timeout handling is not correct. If timeout is large enough and the RX throughput is large enough too, like in my use case above, the ```select``` call will always return with fd being ready. If this happens consistently, like in my use case, you will exit the loop only when the number of bytes read is >= the requested size and the timeout will not play the intended role.

I see this happen for the Cura 2.4 application on latest Ubuntu 16.04 on 2/15/2017.
There are no freezers or compressors involved, just a 3D printer driven by the Printrboard (which is an AVR USB device, different from the Arduinos but related.)
My guess is that this is actually a mis-match between pyserial expectations and kernel driver behaviors.
It's reasonable for character devices to return 0 data for a read request, and I think pyserial should just deal with that.
If you're worried about an infinite read-nothing loop, then detect "no data," usleep for 100 microseconds, and retry. You may even want to use this as a signal to close and re-open the file descriptor, and if re-open fails, then actually die.

You can use it in two ways:
- add a `-f log` to the command line when starting `miniterm` -- you can specify it multiple times so that you will have multiple log files;
- press `ctrl + t ctrl + f` within a session and add a `log` filter (or again, several).

You will then be prompted for log file name to log to.

Examples
========
WARNING: all of those only work with my repo !

##### Example 1
```sh
python -m serial.tools.miniterm --eol CRLF -f log -f default /dev/ttyUSB1 1000000
```
Log everything after `default` filter has been applied on RX data and before on TX data (this is how I understood it was made to be in `miniterm`).

##### Example 2
```sh
python -m serial.tools.miniterm --eol CRLF -f log -f default -f log /dev/ttyUSB1 1000000
```
This will start a `miniterm` session with two log files: one before the `default` filter (cleans some control chars etc...) and one after.
You will be prompted twice for log fil names, in the same order they were given on the command line.

##### Example 3
In a running session started as above, hit `ctrl+t ctrl+f` to edit current filters and enter for instance 'default': this will stop the log(s).
Do it again and enter `log default` to re-enable logging.

@cwhanse thanks a lot for the replies. To answer your questions:

1. geopandas is indeed the underlying structure that's used to build the "registry", where the inputs/outputs and geometries are stored during the calculations. But it's used essentially as a convenient open-source wrapper around pandas that integrates ``shapely`` geometries, the real core package of the model. Originally when I created the algorithm, I was using simple dictionaries and lists. I'm sure there are much smarter and more efficient ways to do this, and it wouldn't be very difficult to change the nature of the registries, and use something more native to Python.

2. you're very right about the overhead though. Using ``shapely`` geometries makes building the PV geometries and calculating the view factors very intuitive and easy to maintain: since the 2D geometry is handled by the package, the view factor formulas look like [these](http://www.thermalradiation.net/sectionc/C-5.html), and you can visualize the PV array and shades on a plot at any point. Besides, the way ``pvfactors`` accounts for reflections is through a linear system of equations for all the surfaces in the PV array. 
But the cost for this is the computation speed: on my machine (i7 processors) it takes about 1 min and a half for 8760  points, and ~50s on a better machine when I was running batch simulations. Apart from model parametrization (which works very well btw), I haven't spent time thinking about replacing ``shapely`` with something that would make the computation faster.

Speed is a top priority for me as well. And maintainability and simplicity are big ones too . 
I was also planning on looking at ``bifacialvf`` because I heard it can run in 4 or 5 seconds, so I'd like to offer some help and contribute to the conversion of that package if that can accelerate its implementation in pvlib.
Let me know!

After some sleep, I see my initial confusion: From my point of view, the time zone is related to the data, not to the location. Maybe it is good to have information on the (legal) time zone of a location, but this is not necessarily an indicator for the time stamp used in e.g. measurement files (and I don't know, if this is clear for a user in general).

Hmm, strange. For me it works as expected. Does your experiment maybe write differently to `info` when loading from a checkpoint?

I definitely like the `--resume` option, and would be onboard with adding that. You are right, in that we can do without a forced resume at first. Would you agree with the following requirments: 

* A `--resume 10` commandline option
* You wanted a `resume=10` argument for the `MongoObserver`, right? Not sure if I like that. It would depend on how we implement it. In my opinion the cleaner way would be to decide "resuming" for the whole run instead of for each observer separately.
* The observer would have to fetch the entry from the database and compare at least config and source code for a match and raise an error in case of a mismatch.
* The observer should then not overwrite `info`, `metrics`, `artifacts` and `stdout` but instead append. 
* It should overwrite `status`, `heartbeat`, `stop_time` and `result` (if any)
* Maybe there should be some entry stating that (and when) the experiment has crashed and resumed.

There are going to be a few difficult bits to implementing this:
* If we want a clean solution with a centralized resume option for the entire run, then the observer interface needs to be adapted.
* Currently the info/stdout are send to each observer and completely replace the old info/stdout. If we want to append, then either the observer needs to take care of merging/concatenating the stored values with the resumed values, or there needs to be a mechanism for the observers to initialize the info/stdout of the experiment.

> Yes, I agree if there is only one build system and if everybody is familiar with it, but those assumption don't hold here. Whether this is "completely standard" in other projects is of no relevance, Z3 is "not standard" in many respects, and yes, we can try make it look "more standard", but that will be a major effort and until we decide to actually go for something like that, I'd rather not disrupt and confuse existing operations and users. So, if it's little bit more effort to make it self-contained and non-disruptive then I think we should go for that.

I don't think the idea of splitting your projects into components and having each component depend on each other is non-standard. But that is exactly how Z3 is organised and what the CMake build system I've created is designed around.

As for a "little bit more effort".... it's not a little bit of effort. It would be a huge amount of effort with no real benefit. CMake is designed to be used in a particular way (the way I'm using it) and what you're suggesting is not a good fit for how CMake works. Also I am not willing to waste my time compromising the readability and maintainability of the CMake build system. I would much rather use my time doing something useful like teaching the CMake build system how to build and install Z3's language bindings which would bring the CMake build system to full feature parity with the Python build system.

Also the Python and CMake build systems can co-exist fairly painlessly. If you're modifying source files you probably won't need to modify the CMake build system. You would only need to modify it if you're adding a new ``.cpp`` file, dependency or build flags or something like that. From what I've observed of Z3's development over the last few months that doesn't seem to happen that often. Even so adding a new source file or component dependency requires only a tiny change to the relevant ``CMakeLists.txt`` file.

> What will `pip install -r requirements.txt` do if I've got a different Theano version installed already?

It will install the version that's in requirements.txt.  If you want to use dependencies as installed on your system, you can just run `pip install Lasagne`, or roll your own requirements.txt, which is useful for bigger projects with lots of dependencies.  Or maybe more interestingly, here's the commands to run both Theano and Lasagne from Git master:

```
git clone https://github.com/Theano/Theano.git
git clone https://github.com/Lasagne/Lasagne.git
cd Theano
python setup.py develop
cd ../Lasagne
python setup.py develop
```

It looks to me this is something very specialised, you can always do it with a custom format.

Here's the hackish, but functional, solution I came up with. Hackish because it relies on the inspect module to get info about the row being imported from the caller frame so that existing M2M relationships can be maintained:
```
class M2MAdditiveCreateWithForeignKey(widgets.ManyToManyWidget):
	def __init__(self, model, m2mfield, separator=', ', field='pk', defaults=None, create=False, *args, **kwargs):
		self.model = model
		self.m2mfield = m2mfield
		self.separator = separator
		self.field = field
		self.defaults = defaults
		self.create = create
		super().__init__(self.model, separator=self.separator, field=self.field, *args, **kwargs)

	def clean(self, value):
		values = filter(None, value.split(self.separator))

		if self.create:
			results = [self.model.objects.get_or_create(defaults=self.defaults, **{self.field:v})[0] for v in values]
			# pdb.set_trace()
			caller_frame = inspect.stack()[1].frame
			caller_args = inspect.getargvalues(caller_frame)
			data = caller_args.locals['data']
			this_row = data[self.field]
			existing_obj = self.model.objects.get(**{self.field:this_row})
			existing_related = getattr(existing_obj, self.m2mfield).all()
			existing_related_objs = [self.model.objects.get(**{self.field:r}) for r in existing_related]
			combined_results = results + list(set(existing_related_objs) - set(results))
			pdb.set_trace()
			return combined_results
		else:
			try:
				val = super().clean(value)
			except ObjectDoesNotExist:
				model_name = self.model.__name__
				error = "Imported data includes nonexistant %s(s) (%s) "\
					"\nThis model does not support creating a new %s when importing data."\
					% (model_name, value, model_name)
				logger.error(error)
				return None
			else:
				return val
```

angr implements a full implementation of the Linux dynamic loader (CLE) so
it is not necessary to have simprocedures for everything. However, diving
deep into libraries for simple IO routines is a great way to get lost
really fast. The solution here will depend on how exactly your test case
fails. If it claims to have exhausted the state space without finding the
correct case, then that’s a bug in either a) our syscall support, b) our
implementation of relocations in CLE or c) our thread-local storage
implementation. If it blows up with a state explosion, then you just need
more simprocedures.

This is not an issue, but mostly an expected output when `LAZY_SOLVES` is enabled. That is because we delay the constraint solving until we have to (e.g. when we resolve the range of a symbolic address), and if the state has already become unsat before that, the constraint solving will fail, and a `SimMemoryAddressError` will be raised. This is a non-issue, but I think the warning message can definitely be improved.

    #EXTM3U

    #EXTINF:420, Rush-"The Garden"-2012 HQ

class CtpEventSource(AbstractEventSource):
    def __init__(self, env, mod_config, md_gateway, trade_gateway):
        self._interval = 60

        self._env = env
        self._mod_config = mod_config
        self._event_queue = Queue() # 所有rqalpha事件都存放到该队列

        self.min_date = datetime.strptime('19700101', '%Y%m%d').date()
        self._before_trading_fire_date = self.min_date
        self._after_trading_fire_date = self.min_date
        self._settlement_fire_date = self.min_date

        # self._md_gateway = md_gateway
        md_gateway.set_event_queue(self._event_queue) # md_gateway将tick事件发送到_event_queue
        trade_gateway.set_event_queue(self._event_queue) # trade_gateway将order/trade相关事件发送到_event_queue

        self._clock_engine_thread = Thread(target=self.clock_worker)
        self._clock_engine_thread.daemon = True

        # self._running = True

    # 利用rqalpha的持久化机制，实现after_trading和settlement事件一天只触发一次
    def set_state(self, state):
        persist_dict = rq_json.convert_json_to_dict(state.decode('utf-8'))
        # self._before_trading_fire_date = persist_dict['before_trading_fire_date']
        self._after_trading_fire_date = persist_dict['after_trading_fire_date']
        self._settlement_fire_date = persist_dict['settlement_fire_date']

    def get_state(self):
        return rq_json.convert_dict_to_json({
            # "before_trading_fire_date": self._before_trading_fire_date,
            "after_trading_fire_date": self._after_trading_fire_date,
            "settlement_fire_date": self._settlement_fire_date,
        }).encode('utf-8')

    def clock_worker(self):
        while True:

            # if is_holiday_today():
            #     time.sleep(self._interval)
            #     continue

            calendar_dt = datetime.now()
            trading_dt: datetime = self._env.trading_dt
            trading_date = trading_dt.date()

            if trading_date > self._before_trading_fire_date:
                event = Event(EVENT.BEFORE_TRADING, calendar_dt=calendar_dt, trading_dt=trading_dt)
                self._event_queue.put(event)
                system_log.debug("calendar_dt {}, trading_dt {}, event {}", event.calendar_dt, event.trading_dt, event.event_type)
                self._before_trading_fire_date = trading_date
            elif calendar_dt >= trading_dt.replace(hour=15, minute=30) and trading_date > self._after_trading_fire_date:
                event = Event(EVENT.AFTER_TRADING, calendar_dt=calendar_dt, trading_dt=trading_dt)
                self._event_queue.put(event)
                system_log.debug("calendar_dt {}, trading_dt {}, event {}", event.calendar_dt, event.trading_dt, event.event_type)
                system_log.debug("calendar_dt {}, trading_dt {}, event {}", event.calendar_dt, event.trading_dt, event.event_type)
                self._after_trading_fire_date = trading_date
            elif calendar_dt >= trading_dt.replace(hour=15, minute=40) and trading_date > self._settlement_fire_date:
                event = Event(EVENT.SETTLEMENT, calendar_dt=calendar_dt, trading_dt=trading_dt)
                self._event_queue.put(event)
                system_log.debug("calendar_dt {}, trading_dt {}, event {}", event.calendar_dt, event.trading_dt, event.event_type)
                system_log.debug("calendar_dt {}, trading_dt {}, event {}", event.calendar_dt, event.trading_dt, event.event_type)
                self._settlement_fire_date = trading_date

            time.sleep(self._interval) # 每60秒检查一次

    def events(self, start_date, end_date, frequency):
        running = True

        self._clock_engine_thread.start()

        while running:
            try:
                event = self._event_queue.get(timeout=1) # Executor.run获取到rqalpha事件进行处理
                yield event
            except Empty:
                pass
`

(2)  any external light (sunlight) has wavelength close to IR wavelength SR300 using will cause interference.

I used this script and **I found its result is not bad.**

```python
import pyrealsense2 as rs
import numpy as np
import cv2
import os

face_cascade = cv2.CascadeClassifier('haarcascade_frontalface_default.xml')

# Configure depth and color streams
pipeline = rs.pipeline()
config = rs.config()
config.enable_stream(rs.stream.depth, 1280, 720, rs.format.z16, 30)
config.enable_stream(rs.stream.color, 1280, 720, rs.format.bgr8, 30)

# Start streaming
pipe_profile = pipeline.start(config)

curr_frame = 0

try:
    while True:

        # Wait for a coherent pair of frames: depth and color
        frames = pipeline.wait_for_frames()
        depth_frame = frames.get_depth_frame()
        color_frame = frames.get_color_frame()
        if not depth_frame or not color_frame:
            continue

        # Intrinsics & Extrinsics
        depth_intrin = depth_frame.profile.as_video_stream_profile().intrinsics
        color_intrin = color_frame.profile.as_video_stream_profile().intrinsics
        depth_to_color_extrin = depth_frame.profile.get_extrinsics_to(
            color_frame.profile)

        # print(depth_intrin.ppx, depth_intrin.ppy)

        # Convert images to numpy arrays
        depth_image = np.asanyarray(depth_frame.get_data())
        color_image = np.asanyarray(color_frame.get_data())

        # find the human face in the color_image
        gray = cv2.cvtColor(color_image, cv2.COLOR_BGR2GRAY)
        faces = face_cascade.detectMultiScale(gray, 1.3, 5)
        for (x, y, w, h) in faces:
            if curr_frame > 100 and curr_frame % 40 == 10:
                roi_depth_image = depth_image[y:y+h, x:x+w]
                roi_color_image = color_image[y:y+h, x:x+w]
                os.system('mkdir -p ./3d_output/%d' % curr_frame)
                cv2.imwrite('./3d_output/%d/depth.jpg' %
                            curr_frame, roi_depth_image)
                cv2.imwrite('./3d_output/%d/color.jpg' %
                            curr_frame, roi_color_image)
                print("the mid position depth is:", depth_frame.get_distance(
                    int(x+w/2), int(y+h/2)))

                # write the depth data in a depth.txt
                with open('./3d_output/%d/depth.csv' % curr_frame, 'w') as f:
                    cols = list(range(x, x+w))
                    rows = list(range(y, y+h))
                    for i in rows:
                        for j in cols:
                            # 坐标变换一定要注意检查
                            depth = depth_frame.get_distance(j, i)
                            depth_point = rs.rs2_deproject_pixel_to_point(
                                depth_intrin, [j, i], depth)
                            text = "%.5lf, %.5lf, %.5lf\n" % (
                                depth_point[0], depth_point[1], depth_point[2])
                            f.write(text)
                print("Finish writing the depth img")

            cv2.rectangle(color_image, (x, y), (x+w, y+h), (255, 0, 0), 2)

         # Apply colormap on depth image (image must be converted to 8-bit per pixel first)
        depth_colormap = cv2.applyColorMap(cv2.convertScaleAbs(
            depth_image, alpha=0.03), cv2.COLORMAP_JET)

        # Stack both images horizontally
        images = np.hstack((color_image, depth_colormap))

        # Show images
        cv2.namedWindow('RealSense', cv2.WINDOW_AUTOSIZE)
        cv2.imshow('RealSense', images)
        cv2.waitKey(1)

        curr_frame += 1
finally:

    # Stop streaming
    pipeline.stop()

```

It's hard to diagnose the problem without more information about the environment. Here's some general pointers for debugging this kind of explosion:

 * How large are the rewards, and are rewards normalized?
 * Does the problem still happen if you use a much larger batch size?
 * How does the environment treat incoming actions, e.g.
   * Does it clip actions if they are too large?
   * Does it penalize or reward large actions?

It is sometimes difficult to get RL algorithms to learn a good set of action standard deviations. If possible, I'd recommend trying to discretize the action space.

2 issues with that for me:
1. I try to keep/maintain as few different pieces/type-of-software as possible. Ex: I use uwsgi with http-enabled and don't use nginx for the app.
2. Nginx still doesn't have a type of distributed caching backend as far as I know. So you have x nginx machines to proxy (either 1 in each app-server, 1 in each solr-server, or separate ones), each would have it's own local cache. And nginx still would have ~random load balancing (~just like calling that shuffle_hosts() before making a request).

Smart client ~is the way to go. Look at ~most nosql: cassandra,hbase,mongodb. Will matter a lot less when you do distributed-search though, since you'll hit ~all shards.

We keep the messages in NCP spinel buffer until we can deliver them to host. I think this is the desired behavior for NCP build. NCP build relies on the host. Host can go to sleep/low-power mode and then we have `otPlatWakeHost()` mechanism and `SPINEL_PROP_HOST_POWER_STATE ` from OpenThread to wake host (when there is a message for host).

@tomas-c, thanks for raising this issue.  Passing the ACK frame up is required for OpenThread to process the Frame Pending bit in an IEEE 802.15.4 ACK frame.  Note that this mechanism is only required when operating as a Sleepy End Device (SED).  In any case, the ACK frame should be passed up to OpenThread for completeness. Hi @kaipak , thanks for the issue.  I recommend one of two solutions to help track this down:

### Create a minimal example

It would be useful to take your current example and remove as much as possible from it while still maintaining the exception.  For example if you take away the entire class then, from what I understand, things work.  How about if you take away some of the methods or attributes?  Do things still break or do they work ok?  I think that if you try taking away different parts from your example you may be able to find a particular piece that is causing problems.  

More general thoughts on this topic in this blog: http://matthewrocklin.com/blog/work/2018/02/28/minimal-bug-reports

This would be my first recommendation.  It's also a good practice to get used to.

### Use pdb and look through the pickle stack trace

Pickle is having trouble serializing a lock, this is not surprising because locks aren't serializable (They won't make sense when they get unserialized).  So you could run this in ipython and then use the `%debug` magic to walk up the stack trace (using `up`) and print the object that is being pickled.  What is holding onto the lock?  What is holding onto that object?  Eventually as you climb up the stack you *might* find some object that you recognize and can easily control.

### Dask handles objects

Just to be clear, dask is perfectly happy to move around normal python objects as long as they are serializable with cloudpickle.  In this case one of those objects has a thread lock, which stops it from being serializable.

@sfdye django-anymail does *not* by itself send emails asynchronously (in the background or on a separate thread from the calling code). If you are sending emails from a Django view -- in response to a user action -- then best practice is to move the send off of the main thread, so that you don't delay the response to the user if the Email Service Provider's send API takes a long time.

Different Django projects have different approaches to async task scheduling, so django-anymail doesn't include any async code of its own. Celery is very popular with Django; if you're already using it in your project for other reasons, it's a good choice for email, too. You can combine Celery with Anymail using the code discussed in this issue, or by using the [django-celery-email](https://pypi.python.org/pypi/django-celery-email) package.

Another background task manager for Django is [django-channels](https://channels.readthedocs.io/en/latest/).

If you're not using Celery or Channels, a simpler way to go async might be [django-mailer](https://pypi.python.org/pypi/django-mailer). It uses your existing Django database to manage the send queue, and you set up a cron job to periodically process the queue from a separate process.

Your async sending code can also handle any errors from calling the ESP's API. There's a bit more discussion of this in the [Anymail docs](https://anymail.readthedocs.io/en/stable/tips/transient_errors/).

>> when returning an rvalue Eigen concrete type (e.g. MatrixXd)
> I'm absolutely not sure here but wouldn't that be an lvalue?

No, for ordinary object returns such as `MatrixXd foo() { return MatrixXd::Random(5, 5); }`; we capture the `MatrixXd` as an rvalue and move it into a new temporary `MatrixXd`.

> In that case, why "when returning a reference (e.g. const MatrixXd &) we copy by default"? (Just curious or maybe I don't fully understand - probably there's a perfectly valid reason for it).

Mainly because that's how lvalue reference returns work in the rest of pybind (with the default `return_value_policy::automatic`).  The justification is that we really can't know how long the lvalue reference is going to stay valid, so you need to take an extra step in the binding code to say "no, really, it's safe to keep using this" by explicitly binding with `return_value_policy::reference` or "no, really, it's safe to keep using this as long as you also keep the object itself alive" by using `return_value_policy::reference_internal`.

> Do I understand correctly that passing a np-array to a C++ function that takes a MatrixXd& or const & would occur a copy? So in this case the general advice would be to change a C++ API to use Eigen::Ref instead of & / const &? I guess you've thought about that and there's a reason for it but is it not possible to get the same behaviour with just normal references, without having to resort to Eigen::Ref's everywhere?

Yes, you're correct.  The issue is that in order to provide a `MatrixXd` instance to a function, we have to *have* a `MatrixXd` instance in the first place, but we don't, we just have a chunk of data in memory with a map of how to interpret that data.  `MatrixXd`, on the other hand, is a concrete class that always owns its own data: being able to reference some other data is the entire point of `Ref<MatrixXd>`.  This is no different than Eigen itself, though: if you try to pass something that isn't exactly a MatrixXd to a function taking a `const MatrixXd &`, you'd invoke a copy.  For example, while you couldn't pass a Matrix4d without a copy, you *can* pass the `Matrix4d` to a `Ref<const MatrixXd>` without needing a copy.

> What happens with objects containing Eigen matrices ...

That won't be a problem at all: pybind11 internally works by storing a C++ instance of your my_class inside the wrapper that is exposed to Python; when a method is invoked, we call the C++ method on that internal instance; no copy is needed.

Definitely a bug (and only occurs with exactly 2 arguments in the initializer list; with 1 or 3 it's fine).

It's caused by the arithmetic constructor in `any_container`: so {1, 2} could either be a implicit construction of an `array_t({shape={1}, strides={2} })`, or an implicit construction of an `any_container` with the initializer list.  You can fix it with `py::array_t<float> array({{n, m}})`, but that's not a nice solution.

I think taking out the arithmetic constructor makes the most sense here, so `any_container` needs a container (or initializer_list), but won't accept a single scalar value.  That was convenient, but not at the cost of introducing ambiguity.

> My use case (and I imagine the use case of many others) is that I have a number of existing C++ libraries with fairly complete documentation in the header files. I want to be able to access that documentation directly from Python (or IPython, with the nifty "?" syntax). Writing the whole documentation again for Python would be unacceptable. Instead, I use a Python helper program (gendoc.py) that calls clang to extract the documentation automatically, which is nice because the Python docs are always up-to-date.

I have been looking at reusing content from header documentations, but Python and C++ documentation systems are hard to reconcile.

Python documentation is an attribute of the objects documented. C++ documentation is part of the code and not included  in any way in the compiled objects. I looked a bit to see if tools like doxygen could generate rst files to be picked up by the python wrappers and inserted in the docstrings, but did not find anything practical.

Finally, I think that the paradigms of the languages are so far from each other that the python-exposed documentation should not be the same as the header documentation, especially when it comes to overloads. Python has not notion of type matching when there are multiple overloads of functions, and the pybind11 mechanism will just use the first one that works.

I experimented with this (see the commit referenced above) by changing pybind to always initialize the holder class.  This passes all the tests except one, and makes the above work.

The one that didn't pass (I removed it as part of the commit) is from test_issues and mentions specifically not initializing the trampoline when not inherited from by a python type.  I'm not sure that this is entirely desirable (nor is it what I expected): there are valid cases for using a trampoline *other* than virtual methods; one I've run into a few times is wanting to expose `protected` methods that I can't access, and occasionally to provide a method or two that wrap the C++ method interface in some more pythonic way.  (As long as the class doesn't have any members, these work: a `type` pointer will be the same as a `type_alias` pointer anyway).

Another use case is to be able to use constructors and destructors to do instance cleanup related to the pybind11 interface layer.  In #327, for instance, where he/she wanted a `__del__`, a less intrusive solution than using the `C` destructor would have been to declare a trampoline class and use its destructor.

So even if it's easily fixable to have alias initialization-when-inherited-from-in-python working again, I think it would be nicer to simply always initialize the alias, allowing the trampoline to do more than it currently can.

Thoughts?

I did some digging and the issue is a conflict between pip-installed packages and yum-installed packages. The `python2-certbot` RPM package has a dependency on `python2-acme`, which depends on `python2-requests`, which depends on `python-urllib3`. If urllib3 has already been installed with `pip`, the installation of `python-urllib3` fails with this error:
```
  Installing : python-urllib3-1.10.2-5.el7.noarch                                                                               19/50
Error unpacking rpm package python-urllib3-1.10.2-5.el7.noarch
error: unpacking of archive failed on file /usr/lib/python2.7/site-packages/urllib3/packages/ssl_match_hostname: cpio: rename
  Installing : python-requests-2.6.0-1.el7_1.noarch                                                                             20/50
error: python-urllib3-1.10.2-5.el7.noarch: install failed
```
This is caused by the `python-urllib3` package having `packages/ssl_match_hostname` as a symlink to `../../backports/ssl_match_hostname` but the pip package having it as an actual folder with files inside it. I don't know why this doesn't cause the entire transaction to fail, but a few things happen as a result of this. Firstly, as a result of the package failing to install, yum doesn't register `python-urllib3` as having installed.

Secondly, and more problematically, the in-progress installation is not rolled back, leaving the files in an inconsistent state. Files that it were installed before the failure are left in place. In practice, this means `/usr/lib/python2.7/site-packages/urllib3-1.10.2-py2.7.egg-info` and its contents as well as anything semantically before `packages/ssl_match_hostname` inside `/usr/lib/python2.7/site-packages/urllib3` are installed, replacing any existing files, and `/usr/lib/python2.7/site-packages/urllib3/packages/ssl_match_hostname` is created a suffix of `;SOMEHASH`. This renders the urllib3 package unusable, because approximately half of its files are from version 1.10.2 and some of its files are from the version installed by pip (currently 1.23).

As a general rule, if you use Python (or Ruby, Perl, etc) packages that are installed through your distro's package manager, you'll want to avoid using the language's package manager to install packages on a system-wide level, lest you run into conflicts like this. You'll want to either use something like virtualenv or just specify a different installation path when installing things with `pip`, `gem`, etc. I recommend the former, as it sets up various shims and such to simplify the process, and the binaries it creates for installed packages can be symlinked into `/usr/local/bin` directly.

One thing that can work as a workaround is to install any distro-packaged dependencies through the distro first so that the language-provided package manager won't install those, but that may not work if the distro has a version that's too old to meet the requirements of the package you want to install. In this case, for example, `python-requests` in CentOS 7 is version 2.6.0, but `python-neutronclient` requires `requests>=2.14.2`. As a result, `python-neutronclient` is probably best installed inside a virtualenv.

1.) Automation: In non-interactive mode, option 1 in the very right box of your chart does not seem reasonable to me (the `-d` options would just be ignored). So, it is not a good idea to provide a flag to enforce option 1 behavior, because something is already wrong when such a command is run; the user should not have specified `-d` in the first place. My favorite behavior would be to just use option 2 without comment, assuming that non-interactive users know what they are doing. Alternatively, one could produce an error by default, and enforce option 2 using `--force-renewal` (this option already exists, meaning something similar.)

Thinking about it more, even in interactive mode, I think option 1 is not very reasonable; it seems like an input error. In non-interactive mode, we can turn this warning into an error, unless `--force-renewal` is specified.

My initial, untested, guess is that we're incrementing the ref count on the return value one time too many, so  it never gets free'd. In __decompress:

```
py_destination = PyBytes_FromStringAndSize (destination, (Py_ssize_t) destination_written);
```
means py_destination has a ref count of 1. Then, doing:

```
return Py_BuildValue ("O", py_destination);
```

increases the ref count by 1 again. As I say, untested, but I think that's what's going on. We should just return py_destination - the call to Py_BuildValue is unneeded there.

Hi @nkbai , thank you very much for the proposal, capacity for the refund is definitely an issue. First let me check if I understood you correctly and make some general remarks:

> refund transfer will lock tokens that is no necessary. maybe only lock these tokens several seconds.

I believe you're proposing a change to reduce the collateral cost of a refund, one really nice approach that we may integrate is defined in the [sprites paper](https://arxiv.org/abs/1702.05812). The sprites approach has the advantage of drastically reducing the required lock expiration, reducing the collateral cost. The disadvantage is that it requires waiting Δ blocks, as a consequence we get "blockchain speed" for a failed transfer. I believe you want an approach that can be employed off-chain and if required enforced on-chain, so that we don't need to wait Δ blocks, correct?

One important note here: The goal of a Refund message is not to reduce the collateral cost, it's a routing strategy. The use of a Refund by a node effectively says to the previous node "I cannot proceed with the transfer, please try a different route, and here are the tokens back". For this reason we may remove this message type and just use a [mediated transfer sent backwards](https://github.com/raiden-network/raiden/issues/490#issuecomment-291778637).

> In a particular case, refund transfer will result in a failure of the transfer. This will be my focus.

This is true. I want to make sure we are using the same terminology here. In Raiden a user starts a payment, the payment is done with the use of a transfer, which may fail like you said, but the payment can still be finalized with a new transfer. (What I'm trying to say, is that at our API level, a single user call will start a transfer, if this transfer fails, a new one is started with a different route for the user's convenience)

As for your design, we are looking at it. I will come back to you once I understand it better. Keep in mind that we are writing the [path finding service](https://github.com/raiden-network/raiden-pathfinding-service) and the [monitoring service](https://github.com/raiden-network/raiden-monitoring-service). These services require a few protocol changes [[1](https://github.com/raiden-network/spec), [2](https://github.com/raiden-network/raiden-contracts)] to work properly, we will need to understand how your penalization scheme fits with these changes.

Interesting.

FWIW, URLs are supposed to be very specific, and (pardon if I get a bit nerdy here) 

"http://example.com/foo" refers to the "foo" file under the "root" and "http://example.com/foo/" refers to the index file under "/foo". Of course, things get really, really fuzzy with "http://example.com" since that doesn't refer to any file specifically, but defaults to be the index in the root. 

The "aud" refers to the target "audience", it's a bit of a reversal of things and means the intended recipient of the VAPID certificate (rather than the URL of what to pull off of the server). Google (being google) is super pedantic about things and wants to make absolutely sure that you address the "aud" to the exact recipient. Mozilla, frankly, doesn't really care. 

VAPID is about the owner identifying themselves. The most important thing in there is the "sub", since that's how we could get in touch with you if we needed to. We check that "you" are "you" by doing a signature check on the key, and doing a bit of equal magic on the URL. Still, the underlying JWT requirement (which is one of the dependencies for VAPID) requires that:
1) The "aud" match the schema and host of the recipient (so, yeah, not the slash, since that's the index file on the host)
2) The "exp" for that VAPID transaction be no more than 24 hours. (that's just the individual transaction, not the VAPID key you generated. That can live as long as you like.)
3) The "sub" be a valid URL construct. (that's why you use "mailto:bob@example.com" instead of "bob@example.com". I don't know why you'd ever do it, but "http://example.com/contact/bob" would also be valid for the spec, but probably not that useful for the poor ops person trying to contact Bob.)

In short:
So, yeah, having the library "fix" any value could actually make things worse if some provider did something different or changed the "aud" requirement. I think it's absolutely a good idea to check and recheck anything you're sending.

I just want to keep this ticket open to represent my uncomfortableness with the string interpolation feature of SoS, for the following reasons,

1. It changes Python syntax in an implicit way. This does not sound like a big deal because when we write sos extensions and import them to sos, they have to use standard Python syntax.

2. It adds yet another python format string method, which makes things complicated especially with the introduction of format string in Python 3.6.

3. It tends to be slow because of the pure python implementation.

On the pros side, SoS' string interpolation feature has the following advantages:

1. handle most data types in a way usable for scripts
2. the conversion strings make python conversion very convenient.

Yes, but my manual procedure would work too, namely

1. run `sos run script -q pbs -n`, wait until the first task is submitted and is "running" to display the script, Ctrl-C.
2. check the printed script and see if it is correct (if paths are correctly translated).
3. copy the task id to clipboard
3. run `sos execute task_id -q pbs` to submit the job and see if it starts execution, wait another few minutes and see if it continues.
4. run `sos run script -q pbs` to submit all jobs. The already submitted jobs will be running and will not be resubmitted to queue.
5. If things go wrong, check the details of the task with command `sos status task_id -q pbs -v4`

@Kaixhin note that for Automatic Portrait Segmentation for Image Stylization, performing simple rotations actually harms performance of the final model (even if you take care of rotating and cropping the image such that no boundary artifacts are introduced due to rotation).
Random scale and crop helps a lot though, but having a large number of transforms that in the end do not contribute to the end performance (and even hurts performance) seems that might not be worth to it to me, as it can confuse users (who might thought that more transforms = better).

A few links showing that some transforms might not be helpful:
- https://github.com/facebook/fb.resnet.torch/issues/30#issuecomment-210542331
- https://github.com/pytorch/vision/pull/27#issuecomment-277920332

But we can extend the transforms if you think it's worth it.

Let's broadly tackle this as part of 0.17.

> Could this have something to do with my go installation not matching a Pulumi assumption?

Possible.  The go language plugin works by taking the name of your project and just trying to run a binary with that name.  This means two things need to have happened:

1. You need to run `go install` to get your program built and installed.
2. You need to ensure that go's bin folder is on your $PATH.

Out of the box, (2) is not true.  So you'll want to ensure you add it to your path (e.g. export `PATH=$(go env GOPATH)/bin:$PATH`)

You should be able to run `which ahoy-pulumi` and see that a program is found.

If you do have `ahoy-pulum`i on your `$PATH` and things still aren't working. We'll have to continue digging. 

> Is there anyway this error message could be more descriptive to help lead me to the source of the issue?

Assuming the above is the issue, we could be a little more pragmatic in the error (maybe with something like "did you `go install` it?"?

I suspect the easiest place to do this would be in https://github.com/pulumi/pulumi/blob/master/sdk/go/pulumi-language-go/main.go#L95, perahps doing an exec.LookPath to ensure `command` is on the path.  If you're interested in contributing, this might be a good place to start.  But if not, that's great too.

Do let me know if the above helped, if not, we'll keep digging.

Hi @richardliaw. Here's a simple example of problem I am having. Consider the following model:

```python
class FullyConnectedNetwork(Model):
    def _init(self, inputs, num_outputs, options):
        activation = tf.nn.tanh
        is_training = tf.placeholder("bool")
        hidden_layers = [10, 6, 4]
        last_layer = inputs
        for size in hidden_layers:
            last_layer = slim.fully_connected(last_layer, size)
            last_layer = tf.layers.batch_normalization(last_layer, training=is_training)
            last_layer = activation(last_layer)

        output = slim.fully_connected(last_layer, num_outputs)
        return output, last_layer
```

From tensorflow docs (https://www.tensorflow.org/api_docs/python/tf/layers/batch_normalization):

> Note: when training, the moving_mean and moving_variance need to be updated. By default the update ops are placed in tf.GraphKeys.UPDATE_OPS, so they need to be added as a dependency to the train_op...

This is the first issue. I don't have direct access to the training operation used by rllib so I can't add `UPDATE_OPS` as a dependency. Additionally, if I understand correctly, this is made more complicated by the fact that we have 2 networks - actor & critic, so we have 2 separate training ops with 2 separate sets of dependencies (I might be wrong on this one so please correct me if I am wrong).

> training: Either a Python boolean, or a TensorFlow boolean scalar tensor (e.g. a placeholder). Whether to return the output in training mode (normalized with statistics of the current batch) or in inference mode (normalized with moving statistics). NOTE: make sure to set this parameter correctly, or else your training/inference will not work properly.

This is the second issue that I am facing. I need to be able to control the value of `is_training` placeholder but I don't have any control over the feed_dict that's passed into tensorflow by rllib during training/prediction.

```
# single quoted parameter with an equal sign splitting parameter name and value

'--stream-sorting-excludes=>720p,>high'

# two (quoted) parameters with the first one expecting a second one containing the values
--stream-sorting-excludes ">720p,>high"

"--stream-sorting-excludes" ">720p,>high"
'--stream-sorting-excludes' '>720p,>high'
```

You can definitely serve web and websocket connections on the same port. This is the reason why WebSocket connection begin with a HTTP handshake that is then upgraded to a full websocket connection. However, your web server does need to support WebSocket upgrades. Many popular web servers have had this support for a while including NGINX. Even websockify has an embedded web server that can turned on and it supports WebSocket upgrades although this is not recommended for production.

In terms of targeting multiple VNC servers, websockify supports a token based system for selecting between multiple targets: https://github.com/novnc/websockify/wiki/Token-based-target-selection 

In terms of security model, I would recommend that you only enable WebSocket upgrades for encrypted websocket (wss, port 443) to prevent eves dropping. You also need to consider how you will prevent unauthorized users from guessing or discovering a token and being able to connect via WebSockets to one of your VNC servers. This is security infrastructure that you will need to configure and/or build. Often this is done with a web UI that uses a well know authorization mechanism and then only enables WebSocket upgrades for that connection. Another option is for the web UI to create a short-lived websockify connection token that is removed once a connection is made. Hope that helps.
